后处理刀轨

后处理刀轨

NX 加工应用模块可生成用来对部件进行加工的刀轨。刀轨包含以下内容：

  • 有关刀具运动的信息，如 GOTO 点、进给率和刀轴方向。

  • 名为事件的控制器说明，例如冷却条件、样条控制和刀具更改。

每个控制器都有不同的要求，例如 G 代码和 G 代码的序列，它们都有控制器制造商设置。每台机床都有不同的要求，例如 M 代码、轴、通道、主轴速度性能和进给率限制。该信息大部分不在初始 NX 刀轨中。

输出文件

NX Post 输出文件可能是：

  • 用刀轨验证或仿真控制面板在 NX 中检查。

  • 已加载到控制器，并在车间底面上运行。

  • 完全模拟 VNCK 应用模块，使用 Siemens 840D 控制器的逻辑生成运动、警告等。机床必须配备 Siemens Sinumerik 840D 控制器，并且 VNCK 必须由机床制造商进行配置。

语言支持

NX 支持对话框和输入文本中的 UTF8 文本字符串。

某些加工文件是程序或数据库文件。这些文件仅支持 ASCII 代码集，而且必须为纯文本。

示例包括以下内容：

  • 与后处理相关联的 .tcl、.def、.cdl 和 .dat 文件。

  • 与通用仿真引擎（Common Simulation Engine，简称 CSE）相关联的 .ccf、.mcf 和 .ini 文件。

NX Post - NX 后处理接口

NX Post 是一组程序和文件，可以在加工应用模块中用来后处理 NX 刀轨和 NX 后处理构造器定义的刀架。

可以使用以下任一方法来启动 NX Post：

  • 在工序导航器中右键单击选定的程序或工艺节点，然后选择后处理。

  • 选择一个或多个程序或工序，然后在主页选项卡→工序组中的后处理 。

NX Post 将刀轨数据用作输入，并输出机床控制器可读 CNC 代码。可以通过几种方式定制后处理器，生成所需的输出。已通过脚本编制语言 Tcl 和使用定义文件实现了 NX Post 处理器的可扩展性。

支持的机床包括以下几个类别：

  • 铣床

  • 车床

  • 产品中心，或同时具备车削和铣削功能的机床

  • 线切割机床

可以使用脚本语言 Tcl 扩展后处理器的能力。

下图说明了以机床和控制器可接受的格式后处理刀轨数据时所需的文件、CAM 对象、内部处理器和步骤。

事件生成器、事件处理器 TCL 文件和事件定义 CDL 文件互相依赖。

注释:

要查看 MOM 所生成事件的类型和顺序，请参见 NX/后处理检查工具对话框中的信息。

加工输出管理器和 NX Post 使用了大量的变量和 Tcl 步骤。变量的常规名称格式为 mom_<name>，而程序的常规名称格式为 MOM_<name>。要了解特定变量和程序，打开后处理构造器应用模块，然后选择实用工具→浏览 MOM 变量。

Manufacturing Output Manager (MOM)

The Manufacturing Output Manager, or MOM, is a utility program used by NX Post to generate output from tool path data. NX Post uses the MOM to do these tasks:

  • Start the interpreter.

  • Add data and specify functions to the interpreter.

  • Load Event Handlers.

  • Load Definition files.

Using 后处理构造器

后处理构造器 is an application that provides a graphical user interface for building posts.

后处理构造器 allows you define many aspects of output, including the following:

  • Types of output blocks and word addresses.

  • Sequence of output for the following:

    • Start of program

    • Start of operation

    • End of operation

    • End of program

    • Tool changes

    • Canned cycles

One of the outputs of the 后处理构造器 program is a Tool Command Language (Tcl) script file. With knowledge of the Tcl language, you can extensively customize Tcl scripts.

后处理构造器 currently configures postprocessors for the following:

  • 3-axis milling machines

  • 3-axis mill-turn centers (XZC)

  • Multi-axis mill-turn centers

  • 4-axis milling machines with a rotary table or a rotary head

  • 5-axis milling machines with dual rotary heads or rotary tables

  • 5-axis milling machines with rotary head and rotary table

  • 2-axis lathes

  • 2 and 4-axis Wire EDM machines.

A controller library is provided, with support for several popular controllers, including Siemens Sinumerik controllers.

The following flowchart illustrates the process of building a postprocessor using 后处理构造器:

Post processor development process

Gather machine tool specifications and controller parameters from manufacturer manuals

Test the postprocessor output

Enter parameters in 后处理构造器

Deliver the functional post processor

Files created by 后处理构造器

By default, 后处理构造器 creates all related files for your postprocessor in the directory where you started 后处理构造器.

To use your postprocessor from the menus in NX you must add an entry to your template_post.dat file in your current postprocessor directory.

You can modify and verify example posts for your specific needs. You must obtain a WebKey user name and password to access the site:

  • https://download.industrysoftware.automation.siemens.com/unigraphics/pb_posts/

  • http://webtest.industrysoftware.automation.siemens.com/WebkeyLogin/Authenticate2?url=http://plmwebtest.industrysoftware.automation.siemens.com/postcatalog/index.jsp

Files always created

Post processors that you create with 后处理构造器 have three main files. If your postprocessor name is my_machine_tool, the files created when you save your post are as follows:

my_machine_tool.pui

Contains information needed to create the .tcl file. This file must be present in the same directory as the .tcl and .def files and is refereneced when you edit your postprocessor.

my_machine_tool.tcl

NX Post uses this Tcl-language file to generate NC code blocks for events.

my_machine_tool.def

This file defines machine tool information such as word formats, addresses, and NC blocks. NX Post uses this file to format the output for events that are processed in the Tcl file.

Other files

my_machine_tool.cdl

Appears when you create a user-defined event or a user-defined cycle.

Contains the definition of a dialog box that appears when you add the event from NX. The dialog box is used to gather user input parameters to control your event or cycle.

my_machine_tool_vnc.tcl

Appears when you select the option to create a virtual NC controller or VNC.

Contains the Tcl code needed to support simulation using machine tool driver (MTD) Tcl-based simulation.

Procedures in ugpost_base.tcl

NX Post uses many procedures in the mach resource postprocessor ugpost_base.tcl file to support file I/O and other basic functions.

This file is updated with each NX release.

You should not modify it, either directly or by overriding procedures with Tcl programming. If you change or override the procedures, there is no guarantee that posts will continue to work correctly from one release to the next.

Some critical procedures that may cause problems if modified:

Some procedures in ugpost_base.tcl

proc MOM_start_of_program

proc SPINDLE_SET

proc EQ_is_zero

proc MOM_end_of_program

proc WORKPLANE_SET

proc VEC3_add

proc MOM_start_of_group

proc RAPID_SET

proc VEC3_cross

proc MOM_end_of_group

proc SEQNO_RESET

proc VEC3_dot

proc MOM_before_output

proc SEQNO_SET

proc VEC3_init

proc MOM_opskip_on

proc MODES_SET

proc VEC3_is_equal

proc MOM_opskip_off

proc CHECK_OPER_TYPE

proc VEC3_is_zero

proc MOM_insert

proc MOM_catch_warning

proc VEC3_mag

proc MOM_pprint

proc OPEN_files

proc VEC3_negate

proc MOM_operator_message

proc CLOSE_files

proc VEC3_scale

proc MOM_before_motion

proc LIST_FILE

proc VEC3_sub

proc MOM_first_move

proc LIST_FILE_HEADER

proc VEC3_unitize

proc ASK_DELTA_4TH_OR_5TH axis_no

proc LIST_FILE_TRAILER

proc MTX3_init_x_y_z

proc ASK_SMALLER_OF_4TH_5TH

proc PPRINT_OUTPUT

proc MTX3_is_equal

proc ASK_DELTA_MOVE

proc isset v1

proc MTX3_multiply

proc ASK_FEEDRATE_FPM

proc hiset v1

proc MTX3_transpose

proc ASK_FEEDRATE_FPR

proc fmt v1 f1

proc MTX3_scale

proc SUPER_FEED_MODE_SET

proc CASE v1

proc MTX3_sub

proc FEEDRATE_SET

proc MOM_text

proc MTX3_add

proc CYCLE_SET

proc EQ_is_equal

proc MTX3_vec_multiply

proc CIRCLE_SET

proc EQ_is_ge

proc MTX3_x

proc COOLANT_SET

proc EQ_is_gt

proc MTX3_y

proc CUTCOM_SET

proc EQ_is_le

proc MTX3_z

proc TOOL_SET evt_name none

proc EQ_is_lt

NX/Post Builder dialog box overview

Use the NX/后处理构造器 dialog box to do the following:

  • Define a new post processor in the Create New Post Processor dialog box.

  • Edit an existing post processor.

  • Manage the available forms of help for the other dialog boxes.

The dialog box contains only a menu bar, toolbar, and a cue line. Other dialog boxes appear as you perform tasks related to post processor definition.

Suggestions for your current task appear in the cue line in the NX/后处理构造器 dialog box.

位于何处？

Menu

Start→All Programs→Siemens NX x.x→Manufacturing Tools→Post Builder

NX/Post Builder dialog box options

Menu

Option

Description

File

Post Properties

Opens the Post Properties dialog box, showing the description you entered when you create the post, machine type, kinematics, output units, and controller information that was entered in the Create New Post dialog box, and the save history of the post processor.

Recently Opened Posts

The list contains a list of several recently opened posts.

At the end of the list you can click Manage to open the Recently Opened Posts dialog box, which lets you scroll through more recently open posts list entries, cut and paste to rearrange the list, or open a selected post.

Options

Validate Custom Commands

Turns on or turns off error checking when you import or create custom commands. You have the following specific error checking options:

Unknown Commands

Detects any syntax error in the name of a command.

Unknown Blocks

Detects any references to an undefined block.

Unknown Addresses

Detects any reference to undefined addresses. This can be used to validate commands such as mom_force_once.

Unknown Formats

Detects references to undefined formats.

Backup Post

Available when no post processor is open for editing.

Lets you choose when backup versions of 后处理构造器 files are created.

Backup Original

Backs up the three files for the original post processor in a folder with the post name and the suffix _org. All intermediate versions are overwritten.

Every Save

Backs up the three post processor files every time you save. A series of folders is created with the post name and the suffixes _bck, _bck1, _bck2, and so on.

This is the default setting.

No Backup

Only the most recent version of the three post processor files is retained.

Enable UDE Editor

Available when no post processor is open for editing.

Lets you override or accept the saved setting for whether theCreate Machine Control Event and Create User Defined Cycle dialog boxes are available. You can choose from the following options:

Yes

Enables UDE and user cycle creation one time for the next post processor you open for edit, regardless of how it was saved.

No

Disables UDE and user cycle creation one time for the next post processor you open for edit, regardless of how it was saved.

As Saved

UDE and user cycle creation are available if the Enable UDE Editor check box was selected when the post was created.

This is the default setting.

Language

Lets you select a language for the 后处理构造器 dialog box interface.

Utilities

Edit Template Posts Data file

Opens the template_post.dat file in the ... mach resource postprocessor folder in the Install Posts dialog box, a simple line editor.

You can browse for other data files.

Use this option to control which post processors are available in pertinent NX 加工 application dialog boxes, and to control the order in which they appear.

The Paste button inserts the clipboard contents before the selected line.

Browse MOM Variables

Opens the MOM Variables Browser, a dialog box that lists available variables, default values, possible values, data types, category, and for most variables, a brief description.

Help

Balloon Tip

When on, , shows tips when you move the cursor over selected buttons in the dialog boxes.

When off, , hides the tips.

Context Sensitive Help

When on, changes the cursor to a question mark. You can click many buttons or options to read a brief description.

When off, , the cursor is displayed in its normal shape and you can use 后处理构造器 dialog boxes normally.

User’s Manual

Opens the NX 加工 后处理构造器 documentation, if it is installed on your computer or configured for network access by your computer.

Tcl/Tk Reference Manuals

Open some provided Tcl/Tk documentation, for your convenience. You might find more current Tcl/Tk documentation on the Internet.

Defining a new postprocessor

Use the New command in the 后处理构造器 application to define the following:

  • The name and description of your new post processor.

  • The type of post to be created: Main Post or Units Only Subpost

  • The default output units

  • The type of machine tool for which the post is intended

  • The controller information

You can also choose to enable the user defined event editor and user cycle editing by default anytime the post is opened for edit.

Where do I find it?

Application

后处理构造器

Shortcut button

New

Menu

File→New

Add a new post to the NX menu

You can always browse to your post in NX. If you use a post frequently, you can add it to the list of available postprocessors that appears in the Postprocess dialog box in NX, and you can position it in the list, using the Edit Template Posts Data File dialog box.

  1. In the NX/后处理构造器 dialog box, choose Utilities→Edit Template Posts Data File.

  2. In the Install Posts dialog box, make certain that the path in the Template Posts Data File box points to the template_post.dat file that is in the folder pointed to by your UGII_CAM_RESOURCE_DIR variable.

    If the path is not correct, click Browse and navigate to the correct file.

  3. In the editing window, select the post before which you want your new post to appear.

  4. Click New.

  5. In the Open dialog box, navigate to your new post, select it, and click Open.

  6. (Optional) If you want to reorder the list of posts, use the Cut and Paste buttons.

    When you paste a line, it is always inserted before a selected line in the file.

    The Edit button allows you to modify the path and file name of a post entry in the list of available post processors. Use it only if you have renamed or moved a post.

  7. Click OK to save and close the edited file..

Define a units-only subpost

This procedure works for post processors that were created in or saved in 后处理构造器 version 8 or later.

  1. In the NX/后处理构造器 dialog box, click New .

  2. In the Create New Post Processor dialog box, select Units Only Subpost.

  3. Under Main Post, click Browse.

  4. In the Select a file dialog box, specify the main post on which to base your units-only subpost, and click OK.

    In the Create New Post Processor dialog box, the units are selected automatically. For example, if the main post is in inches, the Millimeters option is selected.

    The Post Name box is unavailable. The name is based on the name of the main post. For example, if the main post is new_post, and the units are inches, the units-only sub-post is named new_post__MM. The convention is to append two underscores and the abbreviation for the units to the selected name.

  5. (Optional) Modify the description.

  6. Click OK to create the post processor in computer memory and open it for edit.

Add a new post to the NX menu

You can always browse to your post in NX. If you use a post frequently, you can add it to the list of available postprocessors that appears in the Postprocess dialog box in NX, and you can position it in the list, using the Edit Template Posts Data File dialog box.

  1. In the NX/后处理构造器 dialog box, choose Utilities→Edit Template Posts Data File.

  2. In the Install Posts dialog box, make certain that the path in the Template Posts Data File box points to the template_post.dat file that is in the folder pointed to by your UGII_CAM_RESOURCE_DIR variable.

    If the path is not correct, click Browse and navigate to the correct file.

  3. In the editing window, select the post before which you want your new post to appear.

  4. Click New.

  5. In the Open dialog box, navigate to your new post, select it, and click Open.

  6. (Optional) If you want to reorder the list of posts, use the Cut and Paste buttons.

    When you paste a line, it is always inserted before a selected line in the file.

    The Edit button allows you to modify the path and file name of a post entry in the list of available post processors. Use it only if you have renamed or moved a post.

  7. Click OK to save and close the edited file..

Create New Post Processor dialog box

Option

Description

Post Name

Sets the file names for the three files that comprise a main post processor.

When you select the Main Post option, the defualt file name is new_post.

When you select the Units Only Subpost option, the file name is automatically added when you specify the main post.

Description

Displays default information for the selected type of machine tool.

Type of post processor

Main Post

Creates a complete post processor that you can select for use with NX Post.

Units Only Subpost

Creates a subpost that you can pair with one or more main post processors to output the following units:

  • Millimeters, if the selected main post is an inch unit post.

  • Inches, if the main post is a millimeter unit post.

Main Post box

Appears when Units Only Subpost is selected.

Sets the main post processor for the Units Only Subpost type of post. You can select any main post processor that is saved in 后处理构造器 8 or later.

Post Output Unit

Lets you specify inches or millimeters as the default units.

Enable UDE Editor

Lets you create user-defined events or user-defined cycles when you edit the post processor, unless you explicitly disable this capability in the Options→Enable UDE Editor menu of the NX/后处理构造器 dialog box by selecting No before you open the post.

If you do not select the Enable UDE Editor check box, you can create user-defined events or user-defined cycles only when you explicitly enable the capability in the Options→Enable UDE Editor menu of the NX/后处理构造器 dialog box by selecting Yes before you open the post.

Include Virtual NC Controller

Available when you select a controller template that includes a virtual NX controller. For example, it is available when you select Fanuc_30i.

You can also use the Virtual N/C Controller tab of the post processor edit dialog box to define a virtual NC controller.

Machine Tool

Lets you specify the type of machine tool from the following supported configurations:

Mill
3-Axis

Three linear axes: X, Y and Z

3-Axis Mill Turn (XZC)

The mill portion of a mill/turn type machine. Outputs standard Cartesian XYZ or polar coordinates X (radius), Z-angle, and C-angle.

4-Axis with Rotary Table

Three linear axes, X, Y and Z, and a rotary indexing or contouring table, A, B or C.

4-Axis with Rotary Head

Three linear axes, X, Y and Z, and rotary indexing or contouring head, A, B or C.

5-Axis with Dual Rotary Heads

Three linear axes, X, Y and Z, and two rotary indexing or contouring heads A, B or C.

5-Axis with Dual Rotary Tables

Three linear axes X, Y and Z, and two rotary indexing or contouring tables A, B or C.

5-axis with Rotary Head and Table

Three linear axes X, Y and Z, a rotary indexing, or contouring fourth axis head A, B or C, and a rotary indexing or contouring fifth axis table A, B or C.

Lathe
2-Axis

Two linear axes, X and Z.

You can use the Turret option on the Machine Tool tab of the post processor edit dialog box to specify a 4-axis lathe with a fixed distance between two turrets.

Wire EDM
2-Axis

Two linear axes.

4-Axis

Basic four-axis output that you must customize for your machine.

Controller

Lets you specify one of the following:

Generic

A generic controller that you can configure.

Library

Lets you select a pre-defined controller configuration.

The Fanuc, Siemens and Heidenhain mill controls have nurbs output defined.

User’s

Lets you select an existing post processor. The initial controller configuration for your new post is based on the controller configuration in the post you specify.

Defining and editing main post processors

The dialog box to define and edit a post processor shows the name of the currently open post processor in its title bar.

The following main tabs are available:

Machine Tool

Lets you display a generic view of the machine tool, set basic kinematic parameters of the machine tool, and set or modify some basic controller capabilities, such as whether to output circular records.

Program & Tool Path

Lets you add code blocks and words, set the format and sequencing for controller words, managed linked posts, and more.

N/C Data Definitions

Lets you modify blocks, words, formats, and word formats; attach user defined event files; and inherit user defined events from other posts.

Output settings

Lets you manage listing file output and listing file content, attach units-only subposts, and more.

Virtual N/C Controller

Lets you create and configure a virtual numeric controller (VNC). Output is compatible only with the Tcl-based machine tool driver, or MTD.

Note:

This is legacy software and will not be covered in this course. Motion during machine simulation is now defined using the common simulation environment (CSE).

位于何处？

Application

后处理构造器

Toolbar

NX/后处理构造器→Open

Menu

NX/后处理构造器→File→Open

NX/后处理构造器→File→Recently Opened Posts

Create New Post Processor dialog box

OK

Machine Tool tab

The Machine Tool tab has options that allow you to configure the kinematics of your machine tool. You can define the limits and direction of axis movements, orientations where applicable, axis multipliers, traversal feed rate, and so on.

The machine tool configuration tree can be divided in as many as 3 sections, depending on the selected machine tool configuration.

Configuration

Available for…

General Parameters

All machines

Fourth Axis

4-axis and 5-axis mills

Fifth Axis

5-axis mills

Default and Restore

The Default button returns you to the state when the post was last saved for the current tab.

The Restore button returns you to the state when you entered the current tab.

General parameters

The following options are available for all machine types.

Display Machine Tool

Click Display Machine Tool to visualize an approximation of your machine tool. You can verify axis orientations. For a four and five axis milling machine you see the address designation, A, B or C, and the current limit settings for each rotary axis.

Pictures are not available for mill-turn and wire-EDM configurations.

Output Circular Record

You can set this option to Yes or No. If set to Yes, the system outputs all circles based on the circle motion parameters. If set to No the system does not output circle blocks (G2 or G3).

Circular record output also depends on the Default Coordinate Mode and Circular Record Mode settings.

Default Coordinate Mode setting

Circular Record Mode setting

Output

Polar

Polar

Polar circles

Cartesian

Cartesian

Cartesian circles

Cartesian

Polar

No circle output

Polar

Cartesian

No circle output

Linear Axis Travel Limit

This option allows you to check travel limits when you import the custom command pb_cmd_check_travel_limits.tcl.

Home Position

This defines the default FROM position if you have not specified a FROM position.

Linear Motion Resolution

This defines the minimum value to which the system rounds all coordinates. This does not necessarily control the format of the coordinate data. To control the format of the coordinate data you must change the number of decimal digits output.

Traversal Feed Rate

This defines the feed rate that the system uses to calculate machine time for rapid motion. The system also uses this value to determine if a linear feed rate move is actually performed at rapid.

Mill specific parameters

Axis Multiplier

Use Axis Multiplier with diameter programming for mill-turn postprocessors.

Diameter Programming

Use Diameter Programming to multiply the X and I words by two, so that they represent diameter instead of radius.

Mirror X axis

Use Mirror X axis to program your part in the fourth quadrant and output it in the first quadrant. X and I words are multiplied by minus one.

Use Mirror Z axis to multiply the Z and K words by minus one.

Initial Spindle Axis

Specify a value for Initial spindle Axis to set the default spindle axis, which determines the initial work-plane of the machine tool.

Mill Fourth Axis and Fifth Axis parameters

The fourth axis dialog box and fifth axis dialog box set the same parameters, with a couple of exceptions that are mentioned in the following list. The default settings work for most machines.

Note:

There is a general restriction in 后处理构造器 that the C axis (XY plane) cannot be the 5th axis of a 5-axis machine tool.

In order to get the correct output for a dual head rotary 5-axis machine tool with a B-axis in the ZX plane attached to a C-axis in the XY plane, you must define the fourth axis as B in the ZX plane, and the fifth axis as C in the XY plane. The machine tool display function will not work correctly for this configuration; however, the NC program output will be correct.

Machine Zero to 4th Axis Center

Available when Fourth Axis is selected.

Sets the distance from machine zero to the center of rotation for the 4th axis.

This value must be specified for the postprocessor to map correctly between the MCS programmed in NX and the machine tool coordinate system.

4th Axis Center to 5th Axis Center

Available when Fifth Axis is selected.

Sets the distance between the centers of rotation for the 4th axis and the 5th axis.

This value must be specified for the postprocessor to map correctly between the MCS programmed in NX and the machine tool coordinate system.

Rotary Motion Resolution (Deg)

Controls the number of digits to which the calculated rotary angles are rounded by the postprocessor.

Angular Offset (Deg)

Sets the value that needs to be added to adjust the rotary angle to 0.0 when the tool axis is (0,0,1).

For example, some Sundstrand head and table machine tools require an offset of –90, because prior to entering the offset, a tool axis vector of (0,0,1) results in output of an angular position of 90.0 degrees for the fourth axis.

Axis Rotation

Defines whether the axis rotates according to the right-hand rule. The rule states that when you hold your right hand with the thumb pointing in the positive direction of the rotation axis, the fingers curl in the positive direction of rotation.

Normal is the default option.

Select Reverse if your table does not rotate according to the right-hand rule.

For example, if you get B90 output when you require B–90, you may need to select Reverse. As always, test the post thoroughly.

Axis Direction

Defines how the rotary table determines whether to rotate clockwise or counterclockwise.

Magnitude Determines Direction

Rotation to a larger angle is always clockwise and rotation to a smaller angle is always counter clockwise.

B–90 and B90 cause rotation to different positions that are 180 degrees apart on the rotary axis.

Typically, the travel limits on this type of table are more than 360 degrees, for example, –9999 to 9999.

Sign Determines Direction

The sign determines only the direction of rotation.

B90 and B–90 cause rotation to the same position on the rotary table.

When you program B90, the table rotates clockwise from its current location to 90 degrees. When you program B–90, the table rotates counterclockwise from its current location to 90 degrees.

Typically, the limits on this table are from 0 to 360.

Axis Limits (Deg)

Sets the minimum and maximum angles that you can program for the axis.

The postprocessor measures the range of travel clockwise from the minimum angle to the maximum angle.

For example, if you have a rotary axis that has 40 degrees of travel, you can enter one of the following sets of values:

  • –20 degrees for the minimum and 20 degrees for the maximum.

  • 340 degrees for the minimum and 20 degrees for the maximum.

This Rotary Axis Can Be Incremental

Outputs axis motion incrementally when you define operation start and end events for incremental output in NX tool paths.

In the Machine Control section of the operation editing dialog box, create start of path events and end of path events for each operation.

For the operation start event, enter:

SET/MODE,INCR

For the operation end event, enter:

SET/MODE,ABSOL

Rotary Axis Configuration dialog box

Under Rotary Axis, click Configure to open the Rotary Axis Configuration dialog box.

4th Axis and 5th Axis

Display the name for the type of axis that you selected in the Create New Postprocessor dialog box: Head or Table.

For each axis, you can set:

  • The plane of rotation, for example, XY, YZ, or ZX.

  • The word leader, for example, A, B, or C.

Max. Feed Rate (DegMin)

Sets the maximum rotary feed rate for the machine tool.

If the amount of rotation during a combined linear and rotary move exceeds the maximum degrees per minute, the post processor reduces the feed rate so that it does not exceed this value.

Pivot Distance

Sets the distance from the head or table rotation pivot point to the spindle gage point.

This option is not valid for the dual rotary type five-axis machine tool.

You can specify this value in either the fourth axis or the fifth axis dialog box; however, there is only one pivot distance on the machine tool.

Axis Limit Violation Handling
Warning

Outputs a warning to the listing file or warning file, if one or both options are active. No corrective action is taken.

Enable warning output on the Output Settings→Other Options tab.

Enable listing output on the Output Settings→Listing File tab.

Retract/Re-Engage

  • Retracts to clearance cylinder or sphere.

  • Repositions the rotary axis to a valid position, if possible.

  • Re-engages to a user-defined distance above the part.

  • Feeds to the previous position.

  • Moves to the programmed end point.

To change the default retract or re-engage distance you must modify the custom command PB_CMD_init_rotary. Modify the following lines:

set mom_kin_retract_plane 10.0
set mom_kin_retract_distance 10.0
set mom_kin_retract_type "PLANE" ;# DISTANCE or PLANE
set mom_kin_reengage_distance .20
User Defined

Lets you click Handler to specify a block with appropriate words or custom commands to execute when a rotary axis limit violation occurs.

Linearization Interpolation

Lets you specify the default linearization tolerance. The tool path is subdivided into moves that are within the specified tolerance. By default, linearization is set to off.

Combined rotary and linear motion causes the motion to deviate from the desired straight line move.

Rotary Angle

(Default) Interpolates points using a legacy algorithm. Use this option if you are cutting with the tool tip only. If you are cutting with the side of the tool, you will see overcut or undercut as the distance along the cutter from the tool tip increases.

Tool Axis

Interpolates points much closer to the plane formed by the start and end vectors of the tool axis. Use this option if you do side-cutting operations.

These 2 methods are comparable to the ORIAXES and ORIVECT (or ORIPLANE) options of the Sinumerik 840D controller.

To activate linearization for four-axis and five-axis milling machines, you can use the post command LINTOL in NX.

Under Machine Control, create a start of path event, User Defined. The syntax for this command is as follows:

LINTOL/ [ON | OFF | n]

When you specify a tolerance value n, it implicitly turns on linearization.

ON turns on linearization with the last specified linearization value.

OFF turns off linearization.

You can also use linearization for mill turn machines to linearize the XZC moves. To use linearization for XZC posts, set the output mode to polar. To do this, use the default setting for the post command: SET/POLAR,ON. The syntax for the command is:

SET/POLAR, [ON | OFF]

ON sets the output mode to polar. The coordinate output is XZC and is linearized if needed.

OFF sets the output mode to Cartesian. The coordinate output is XYZ and no linearization takes place.

Mill Rotary Axis Auto Clamping

Rotary Axis Auto Clamping is a non-dialog box option for Mill posts that allows you to automatically clamp and unclamp the fourth axis and/or fifth axis for all motion events and Rotate UDE events.

  • To activate auto clamping, program a UDE post command CLAMP/AUTO, ON.

  • To turn off auto clamping, program a UDE post command CLAMP/AUTO, OFF.

By default auto clamping is set to OFF. The system outputs the following by:

  • M10 to clamp the fourth axis.

  • M11 to unclamp the fourth axis.

  • M12 to clamp the fifth axis.

  • M13 to unclamp the fifth axis.

If you need different code(s) you can change the output. Under Program and Tool Path → Custom Command, select the custom command PB_CMD_clamp_fourth_axis, PB_CMD_unclamp_fourth_axis, PB_CMD_clamp_fifth_axis or PB_CMD_unclamp_fifth_axis. Change the output lines as required.

Mill Lock/Axis

Four Axis

In this mode you need to select the axis plane (XY, YZ or ZX) you want to lock. This forces the post to recalculate the remaining axis to determine the tool position. You can only use the option when the tool axis is perpendicular to the plane of the fourth axis. This is the (0,0,1) or (0,0,-1) with XY plane, (0,1,0) or (0,-1,0) with ZX plane and (1,0,0) or (-1,0,0) with the YZ plane.

Five Axis

For a five axis post processor, you can only lock an axis in the plane of the fifth axis when the tool is perpendicular to the plane of the fifth axis. After you rotate the fifth axis you can lock one of the linear axis in the locked plane.

All other LOCK/AXIS syntax combinations are for legacy considerations when exporting CLSF.

Mill/Turn Axis Parameters

Circular Record Mode

For a 3-axis mill-turn (XZC) post, the Circular record output also depends on the Default Coordinate Mode and Circular Record Mode settings.

Default Coordinate Mode setting

Circular Record Mode setting

Output

Polar

Polar

Polar circles

Cartesian

Cartesian

Cartesian circles

Cartesian

Polar

No circle output

Polar

Cartesian

No circle output

Initial Spindle Axis

You can specify Z Axis (0,0,1), X Axis (1,0,0), -X Axis (-1,0,0), or Y Axis (0,1,0). If you select Z axis the system only allows a tool axis of (0,0,1). If you select X Axis, -X Axis, or Y Axis the system only allows tool axes in the XY plane.

Position in Y-Axis

This option is only available when the Z component of the tool axis is zero. Use this option for cross drilling when the tool axis does not pass through the centerline.

On

For machines that can position Y in addition to XZC. This mode is only valid when the tool axis has a K component of zero for work on the circumference. When the tool axis does not pass through the centerline, the post will then calculate the Y-axis position.

Off

For machines that can only position in XZC. When the tool axis has a K component of zero and the tool axis does not pass through the centerline, the system will attempt to calculate a valid tool position.

If a machine has a spindle axes of other than (0,0,1) and with a machine that has no Y-axis, the tool may gouge if the tool axis does not pass through the center of rotation. If the tool is not a ball end mill and the tool axis does not go through the center of rotation, it will definitely gouge. If the tool is a ball end mill and the tool axis does not go through the center of rotation, it may gouge if the local contour is a wall or slot next to the tool. The system outputs a warning for all the above situations.

Machine Mode

Simple Mill/Turn

This type of mill turn is mated to a single lathe post. The system uses the operation type to switch between a mill post processor and a lathe post processor. This type of mill turn cannot be linked to any other posts. You don't need to add UDEs to the tool path to cause the post processor switching to occur between your lathe and mill posts. You can use the Linked Post section to define the Start of Head and End of Head event markers.

XZC Mill

This type of mill/turn is an independent post that can be linked to any other post.

Name of Lathe Post

If the Machine Mode is the Simple Mill/Turn, you need a lathe post to mate with the current mill post. By default the mating post is lathe_tool_tip in the MACH post processor directory. The mill post is the main post and must be the initial post specified in the template_post.dat file.

Output Mode

Cartesian

Use this option for machines that have an automatic conversion of XY into X and C. For this type of machine the post is output normal XYZ data and the macnine control will do the conversion. On Fanuc controls, this mode is turned on with G13.1.

Polar

Use this option for machines that do not have a built in conversion to X and C. The post outputs all motion as ZXC. The system Linearizes all straight moves into a series of small moves to stay within tolerance. On Fanuc controls, turn this mode on with G12.1.

C Axis Parameters

Fourth Axis Parameters

Radius output (Rotary Axis parameters)

You can choose one of the following options to control output of the X-axis motion.

Shortest Direction

The post minimizes C axis travel.

Always Positive

The system adjusts C to insure X is always positive.

Always Negative

The system adjusts C to ensure X is always negative.

2-axis lathe-specific parameters

Output Method

Defines the tool tracking method.

Tool Tip

Makes the X-values and Z-values in your NC output file represent the tool tip location. Resets the tool tip location with every tool change.

The basic elements for tool tip programming are:

  • A FROM command at the beginning of the program to define the initial turret reference position.

  • A G92 block with each tool change to take into account the change in offsets.

  • A GOHOME or return to the home position to return to the turret reference position.

Turret Reference

Makes the X-values and Z-values in your NC output file represent the turret reference location, similar to the method for milling machine programs.

Turret

Defines whether you have one or two turrets.

One Turret

The default option.

Two Turrets

Currently, when you select this option, the turrets must be a fixed distant apart and may not move independently.

becomes available when you select Two Turrets.

Configure

Available when you select Two Turrets.

You must select the names of the two turrets from lists of recognized names, and you must specify the X-distance and Z-distance between them. The postprocessor adds the offset values to the X-axis and Z-axis coordinates when the secondary turret is used. The distance must be measured from the primary turret to the secondary turret.

Axis Multipliers

Specifies that the machine requires diameter programming. You can also use it if your shop practice is to use diameter programming.

Diameter Programming

Outputs the X-word as a diameter. When you select 2X, then 2I becomes available. Select 2I if your machine also requires the I-value to be multiplied by two.

Mirror Output

Lets you program your part in other quadrants and output in the first, or positive, quadrant.

Select the combination of X, I, Z, and K multipliers that you require. For each word selected, the corresponding value is multiplied by negative one in the output.

线切割 specific parameters

Wire Tilt Control

Defines the method to control the upper wire guide.

Angles

Outputs tilt and lead relative to the direction of motion. This is typical output for Agie controllers.

Coordinates

Outputs upper wire guide coordinates relative to the X and Y position of the lower wire guide. This is typical of the output for Mitsubishi and Charmilles controllers.

Change post processor kinematics

You can start with any existing post processor created with 后处理构造器 and change the kinematics or the inch/metric setting through the following process:

  1. Make a new post.

  2. Select the new inch or metric setting.

  3. Select the new kinematics setting.

  4. Select the user option on the controller to browse for the post you want to change.

  5. Create the post.

Your new post now has all of the custom commands and edits from your original post, but with new kinematics and/or inch/metric settings.

You may need to add rotary words to the motion blocks when making a four or five axis post from a three axis post. To do this follow the steps below:

  1. Go to Program & Tool Path→Program→Motion. In this dialog you may need to change the Linear Move and Rapid Move blocks. To do this, follow the steps below:

    1. Click on the Linear Move, or Rapid Move blue event marker.

    2. In the dialog that displays, select the down arrow next to the drop down menu.

    3. From the drop down menu select fourth_axis.

    4. Click and hold the Add Word button until a block displays.

    5. Drag the block over the block until a white outline appears, than release the mouse.

    If you have a four axis post you may only need to add the fourth_axis word. If you have a five axis post you may need to add both the fourth_axis and fifth_axis.

  2. Go to Program & Tool Path→Program→Canned Cycles and select the Common Parameters event marker. Add the fourth_axis word in the same manner as the Linear and Rapid Move event markers.

Program & Tool Path tab

The Program &Tool Path tab has options that allow you to determine the NC output for specific events and commands that are generated by the NX Event Generator. The tabs under this option control the content, format and order of output for your NC Code.

Program tab

后处理构造器 breaks down your NC program into five distinct sequences. Each one of the sequences allows you to control its output.

Some instructions, such as the rewind stop code and program end, are required at the start and end of NC programs.

Other instructions, such as tool change sequences, starting and turning off the spindle, turning the coolant on and off, and applying tool offsets, are required at the start and end of operations.

Instructions for other events, such as linear motion, circular motion, and so on, may apply in each tool path sequence.

Program Start Sequence

Operation Start Sequence

Tool Path

Machine Control

Motion

Canned Cycles

Miscellaneous

Operation End Sequence

Program End Sequence

Sequence structure

The right side of the window displays the structure of the sequence that you select in the sequence tree at the left.

Program sequence structure tree

Selected sequence markers

Selected sequence blocks

The yellow markers correspond to events in the tool path.

The code blocks correspond to the following:

  • Normal code block

  • Custom or MOM command

  • Operator Message

  • Custom macro function

If a code block is used in more than one sequence, it has a light blue background.

If a code block is used only in the sequence where you see it, it has a white background.

Output conditions

Use the Output Condition command to create, edit, or remove custom output conditions for a block or macro. For example, you can write Tcl code to enable or suppress output under specified conditions, or to force the block output into two lines of code. In previous releases you could only do this by editing the Tcl program.

You can also prevent the code sequence numbers from appearing in the output for a block or macro by using the Suppress Sequence Number command.

Default appearance of a block

Block with sequence numbers suppressed

Block with an output condition

Block with sequence numbers suppressed and an output condition

Appearance on the Program tab, where you can create, edit, or remove conditions.

Program Start Sequence

This sequence has only one marker, the start of program. This window allows you to determine which blocks the system outputs when this event occurs in the tool path. It is important to note that the start of program event occurs before any other event is handled. Any UDE (User Defined Event) data is only available after the Start of Program Event.

Note that the Start of Program and End of Program events are only output once by the main post of linked post. If you overwrite either of these event handlers, the linked post reinstates the original Start of Program and End of Program.

Operation Start Sequence

This sequence defines the blocks that the system can output at the start of each operation. Every operation has a first tool change, automatic tool change, manual tool change or no tool change. See the list of scenarios below for the events output with each scenario.

The Operation Start Sequence has the following Markers:

Start of Path

The system initially generates and continues to generate this event when you post process an operation. This event occurs whether or not there is a tool change.

From Move

This event only occurs if you have programmed a FROM in the operation. If you attach a block to this marker, the system only outputs the block if there is a FROM in the operation.

First Tool

This event only occurs for the first tool change in a program. It is useful if you already have a tool in the spindle and you wish to handle this event differently. If this marker has no blocks attached to it, the blocks the system outputs are the same as the automatic tool change block.

Automatic Tool Change

This event occurs only if the operation contains a tool change. NX triggers a tool change only when the tool is different between the current operation and the previous operation. Note that this event is not related to the post processor command LOAD/TOOL. This event occurs before the system outputs the post processor command LOAD/TOOL. You cannot use any of the LOAD/TOOL data with this event.

Manual Tool Change

This event occurs only if there is a manual tool change. NX triggers a tool change only when the tool is different between the current operation and the previous operation.

Initial Move

The system generates this event only if the operation has a tool change. It creates this event if there is no positioning move after the tool change. The system triggers all Start Post User Defined Events before the initial or first move in an operation. This event is the best place to handle the data triggered by operation level UDEs.

If there is a drilling operation that contains no avoidance moves after a tool change, the post creates an initial move before the cycle. This is to allow the tool length compensation to be applied on the first Z move after the tool change.

First Move

This event only occurs if there is no tool change in the operation. Every operation has either a first move or an initial move. These events allow you to combine blocks either before, with, or after the first motion block of the operation. If the program contains no motion before the canned cycle, the system creates a motion block to position the tool to the rapid clearance plane above the hole.

Approach Move

This event only occurs if the operation contains an approach move. The system only outputs events attached to this event if the operation contains an approach move. The system outputs any blocks attached to this event before the actual approach move motion block.

Engage Move

This event only occurs if the operation contains an engage move. The system only outputs events attached to this event if the operation contains an engage move. The system outputs any blocks attached to this event before the actual engage move motion block.

First Cut

This event occurs before the first cut move. The system outputs any blocks attached to this event before the actual first cut move motion block.

Start of Pass

This event occurs at the start of every roughing or finishing wire edm pass. Use this event to add information (power, offsets, flushing, etc.) that is different on each pass.

Cutcom Move

If Cutcom is active, this event occurs after the start of pass event. Use this event to specify the NC code needed to initiate cutter compensation.

Lead In Move

This event occurs only when you specify a lead in move in NX.

Machine Control

There are two types of events on the Machine Control tab:

  • System machine control events are displayed in light blue . You cannot create new events of this type, nor can you delete the existing ones.

  • Custom machine control events are displayed in green . You can create and delete events of this type.

When you create a new event, the event definition is added to the .cdl file, and the event handler is added to the .tcl file.

Machine control events control the machine tool devices such as coolant, spindles, tail stocks or clamps.

You can use machine control events to change modes such as incremental, absolute, inverse time, per minute, per revolution or constant surface speed.

Machine control information is sent to your post processor in two ways:

Parameter data

Information that you specify in NX as operation parameters available at the start of operations. This includes feeds and speeds data, tool data, and cutting parameters such as stepover, tolerances, and so on.

User Defined Events (UDE) or post commands

You can specify user-defined events at the start and end of programs, and at the start and end of operations. These events are output in the order of operation markers in 后处理构造器.

We recommend you collect available data types such as spindle speed, spindle direction, coolant status, length compensation, tool number, and so on and output it as part of the operation start sequence in NX.

Legacy operations offer a choice to use user-defined events to specify all of the machine control information. These legacy operations are still supported; however, we recommend that you use the user-defined events only for data not available in the operation start and end sequences.

Edit an existing event

Right-click the event and choose Edit User Defined Parameters.

Create a new event

Right-click in the background and choose Create New Machine Control Event.

位于何处？

Menu

Options→Enable UDE Editor→Yes (once for each post)

Location in dialog box

Program & Tool Path tab→Program tab

Machine Control tab

Tool Change

Click the Tool Change event marker to specify the following:

M Code

Lets you specify the code for a tool change in the Tool Change box. The default value is 6, to create M6, M06, and so on, depending on your word format.

Tool Number

Lets you specify the maximum and minimum tool numbers that the controller and machine tool recognize.

T Code

Click Configure to select one of the following options:

  • Tool Number Only, for example, T01.

  • Tool Number and Length Offset Number, for example, T0102 for tool 1 and offset register 2.

  • Length Offset Number and Tool Number, for example, T0201 for tool 1 and offset register 2.

These options are duplicated in the Length Compensation event.

Time (Sec)

Lets you specify the time in seconds for a tool change. This number is added to the total machining time calculation, mom_machining_time, at every tool change.

You can configure the M word format on the M Codes or Word Summary tab.

You can configure the T word format on the Word Summary tab.

Length Compensation

Click the Length Compensation event marker to specify the following:

G code

Lets you specify the G code that corresponds to the cutter length adjustment event.

Length Offset Register

Lets you specify the minimum and maximum length offset register that the controller recognizes.

T Code

Click Configure to select one of the following options:

  • Tool Number Only, for example, T01.

  • Tool Number and Length Offset Number, for example, T0102 for tool 1 and offset register 2.

  • Length Offset Number and Tool Number, for example, T0201 for tool 1 and offset register 2.

These options are duplicated in the Tool Change event.

You can configure the existing G and H words and word formats as required, and add relevant new words if necessary.

Set Modes

The Set Modes event does not have a default output word associated with it and by default produces no output. Typically, G90 and G91 codes are output in a block with motion.

If you require that mode output must be in a separate block, specify one of the G_mode words in this event. For example, use the G90 – Absolute/Incremental Mode word to output G90 when you program the user-defined event SET/MODE,ABSOL in NX, and G91 when you program the user-defined event SET/MODE,INCR in NX.

Click the Set Modes event marker to specify the following:

Output Mode

Specify which of the following configurations for which the controller requires a G code:

  • Both absolute and incremental modes.

  • Absolute mode only.

  • Incremental mode only.

G Code

Lets you specify the absolute and incremental mode G codes. The defaults are G90 and G91, respectively.

Spindle RPM

The recommended method of output is to include the spindle start information in the operation start sequence a the tool change or initial move in NX.

If a user-defined event turns on the spindle, then the blocks defined in this dialog box are output.

Click the Spindle RPM event marker to specify the following:

Spindle Direction M Codes

Lets you define the NC code needed to start the spindle in RPM mode.

Spindle Range Control

Lets you set the time needed to change the speed range, for machining time calculations.

Click Specify Range Code to open a dialog box that lets you specify one of the following types of range code.

None

Use this option for machine tools that automatically determine the range, have only a single spindle range or control the range manually.

Separate Range M Code

Use this option to specify a list of M codes to output for each range, and the minimum and maximum speeds for each range. You must place the M_range word M41 – Separate Range M Code (M41, M42, ...) into the output sequence. The M_range words appear in the word list under More.

High/Low Range with S Code (S+100)

Use this option to output the range code with the spindle speed code. You can use two ranges. For range 1 and spindle speed 100, the S code is S100. For range 2 and spindle speed of 500, the S code is S-500.

Range Number with Spindle M Code (M13)

Use this option to output the range code with the spindle direction code. The M code is calculated as the range code * 10 + the spindle direction code. For example, for range 1 and spindle direction of CLW, usually M3, the value calculated is 1*10 + 3 ad the output is M13. For range 3 and spindle direction CCW, the output is M34.

You can configure the existing S and M words and word formats as required, and add relevant new words if necessary.

Spindle Off

Click the Spindle Off event marker to specify the M code to turn off the spindle.

To get M05 output, you must do one of the following:

  • Add a Spindle Off event to the Object→End Events commands NX.

  • Add an M05 word to a sequence that iis always executed, such as the Operation End Sequence.

Output spindle start with motion on first rapid motion block

Unless you change the default settings, a mill post outputs the block format shown in the following example:

T M06
T
G00 G90 X Y S M03

Output spindle start before first rapid motion block

This example shows how to obtain the following output format:

T M06
T
S M03
G00 G90 X Y

  1. On the Program & Tool Path→Program→Operation Start Sequence tab, under the Start of Path marker, from the PB_CMD_start_of_operation_force_addresses custom command block, remove S M_spindle.

    The edited line should resemble the following code:

    MOM_force once X Y Z fourth_axis fifth_axis F

  2. Add the block spindle_rpm to the Initial Move and First Move markers.

    Every operation has either a first move or an initial move, but never both.

    3. The resulting output resembles the following:

    T02 M06
    T03
    S500 M03
    G00 G90 X2.5909 Y3.0723

Output spindle range code in motion, S, and M03 block

This example shows how to obtain the following output format:

T M06
T
G00 G90 X Y S M03 M41

  1. On the Program & Tool Path→Program→Tool Path→Machine Control tab, click the Spindle RPM block to edit it, and in the Event – Spindle RPM dialog box, click Specify Range Codes.

  2. In the Spindle Range Code Table dialog box, select the range code method that applies to your machine and controller, and enter the appropriate minimum and maximum for each range.

    Do not overlap range values.

    Leave unused boxes set at 0 minimum and 0 maximum.

    For example, select Separate Range M Code (M41), and enter values for code 41, 42, and so on.

  3. When you have entered all values in the Spindle Range Code Table dialog box, click OK.

  4. In the Event – Spindle RPM dialog box, click OK.

  5. On the Custom Command tab, click Import, navigate to the default {$UGII_BASE_DIR} POSTBUILD pblib folder, select pb_cmd_feeds_speeds_utils.tcl, and click Open.

    By default, all of the procedures in the library are selected.

    If all of the procedure check boxes are not selected, click Select All.

  6. Click OK.

  7. On the Program→Operation Start Sequence tab, from the list, select PB_CMD_spindle_rpm_range_select and drag it to both the Initial Move and First Move event markers.

    Every operation has either a first move or an initial move, but never both.

  8. On the Operation Start Sequence tab, under the Start of Path marker, click the custom command PB_CMD_start_of_operation_force_addresses to edit it.

  9. In the Tcl command, add the M_range parameter, as shown.

    MOM_force once S M_spindle M_range X Y Z F

  10. If the range requires an M code, as shown in this example, on the Tool Path→Motion tab, under the Rapid Move marker, add the M_range (M41 – Separate Range M Code (M41, M42, ,,,) word to the rapid longitudinal axis and transversal axis block.

    11. The resulting output resembles the following:

    T03 M06
    T04
    G00 G90 X2.5909 Y3.0723 S100 M03 M41
    ...
    T04 M06
    T05
    G00 G90 X2.5909 Y3.0723 S500 M03 M42
    ...
    T05 M06
    T06
    G00 G90 X2.5909 Y3.0723 S800 M03 M43

Output spindle range combined with spindle start code in motion block

This example shows how to obtain the following output format:

T M06
T
G00 G90 X Y S100 M13

  1. On the Program & Tool Path→Program→Tool Path→Machine Control tab, click the Spindle RPM block to edit it, and in the Event – Spindle RPM dialog box, click Specify Range Codes.

  2. In the Spindle Range Code Table dialog box, select the range code method that applies to your machine and controller, and enter the appropriate minimum and maximum for each range.

    Do not overlap range values.

    Leave unused boxes set at 0 minimum and 0 maximum.

    For example, select Range Number with Spindle M Code (M13), and enter values for code 1, 2, and so on.

  3. When you have entered all values in the Spindle Range Code Table dialog box, click OK.

  4. In the Event – Spindle RPM dialog box, click OK.

    Because the range is combined with the M03, there is no need to add a word.

  5. On the Custom Command tab, click Import, navigate to the default {$UGII_BASE_DIR} POSTBUILD pblib folder, select pb_cmd_feeds_speeds_utils.tcl, and click Open.

    By default, all of the procedures in the library are selected.

    If all of the procedure check boxes are not selected, click Select All.

  6. Click OK.

  7. On the Program→Operation Start Sequence tab, from the list, select PB_CMD_spindle_rpm_range_select and drag it to both the Initial Move and First Move event markers.

    Every operation has either a first move or an initial move, but never both.

  8. On the Operation Start Sequence tab, under the Start of Path marker, click the custom command PB_CMD_start_of_operation_force_addresses to edit it.

  9. In the Tcl command, add the M_range parameter, as shown.

    MOM_force once S M_spindle M_range X Y Z F

    10. The resulting output resembles the following:

    T03 M06
    G00 G90 X2.5909 Y3.0723 S100 M13
    T04
    ...
    T04 M06
    T05
    G00 G90 X2.5909 Y3.0723 S500 M23
    ...
    T05 M06
    T06
    G00 G90 X2.5909 Y3.0723 S800 M33

Output spindle range in a block before motion, S, and M03

This example shows how to obtain the following output format:

T M06
T
M41
G00 G90 X Y S M03

  1. On the Program & Tool Path→Program→Tool Path→Machine Control tab, click the Spindle RPM block to edit it, and in the Event – Spindle RPM dialog box, click Specify Range Codes.

  2. In the Spindle Range Code Table dialog box, select the range code method that applies to your machine and controller, and enter the appropriate minimum and maximum for each range.

    Do not overlap range values.

    Leave unused boxes set at 0 minimum and 0 maximum.

    For example, select Separate Range M Code (M41), and enter values for code 41, 42, and so on.

  3. When you have entered all values in the Spindle Range Code Table dialog box, click OK.

  4. In the Event – Spindle RPM dialog box, click OK.

  5. If the range requires an M code, as shown in the example, on the Tool Path?Motion tab, under the Rapid Move marker, add the M_range (M41 – Separate Range M Code (M41, M42, ,,,) word to the rapid longitudinal axis and transversal axis block.

  6. On the Custom Command tab, click Import, navigate to the default {$UGII_BASE_DIR} POSTBUILD pblib folder, select pb_cmd_feeds_speeds_utils.tcl, and click Open.

    By default, all of the procedures in the library are selected.

    If all of the procedure check boxes are not selected, click Select All.

  7. Click OK.

  8. On the Program?Operation Start Sequence tab, from the list, select PB_CMD_spindle_rpm_range_select and drag it to both the Initial Move and First Move event markers.

    Every operation has either a first move or an initial move, but never both.

  9. On the Operation Start Sequence tab, under the Start of Path marker, click the custom command PB_CMD_start_of_operation_force_addresses to edit it.

  10. In the Tcl command, add the M_range parameter, as shown.

    MOM_force once S M_spindle M_range X Y Z F

  11. On the Operation Start Sequence tab, from the list, select New Block.

  12. Drag the new block to the Initial Move marker, after the PB_CMD_spindle_rpm_range_select block, and before the PB_CMD_spindle_rpm_check command block, if it is present.

  13. Click the new block to edit it, and add the M_range (M41 – Separate Range M Code (M41, M42, ,,,) word.

  14. Right-click the new M41 block and choose Copy As?Referenced Block(s).

  15. Under the First Move marker, right-click the PB_CMD_spindle_rpm_range_select block and choose Paste?After.

    Every operation has either a first move or an initial move, but never both.

    16. The resulting output resembles the following:

    T03 M06
    M41
    G00 G90 X2.5909 Y3.0723 S100 M03

Output spindle range, S, and M03 in a block before motion

This example shows how to obtain the following output format:

T M06
T
S M03 M41
G00 G90 X Y

  1. On the Program & Tool Path→Program→Tool Path→Machine Control tab, click the Spindle RPM block to edit it, and in the Event – Spindle RPM dialog box, click Specify Range Codes.

  2. In the Spindle Range Code Table dialog box, select the range code method that applies to your machine and controller, and enter the appropriate minimum and maximum for each range.

    Do not overlap range values.

    Leave unused boxes set at 0 minimum and 0 maximum.

    For example, select Separate Range M Code (M41), and enter values for code 41, 42, and so on.

  3. When you have entered all values in the Spindle Range Code Table dialog box, click OK.

  4. In the Event – Spindle RPM dialog box, click OK.

  5. If the range requires an M code, as shown in this example, on the Tool Path→Motion tab, under the Rapid Move marker, add the M_range (M41 – Separate Range M Code (M41, M42, ,,,) word to the rapid longitudinal axis and transversal axis block.

  6. On the Custom Command tab, click Import, navigate to the default {$UGII_BASE_DIR} POSTBUILD pblib folder, select pb_cmd_feeds_speeds_utils.tcl, and click Open.

    By default, all of the procedures in the library are selected.

    If all of the procedure check boxes are not selected, click Select All.

  7. Click OK.

  8. On the Program→Operation Start Sequence tab, from the list, select PB_CMD_spindle_rpm_range_select and drag it to both the Initial Move and First Move event markers.

    Every operation has either a first move or an initial move, but never both.

  9. On the Operation Start Sequence tab, under the Start of Path marker, click the custom command PB_CMD_start_of_operation_force_addresses to edit it.

  10. In the Tcl command, add the M_range parameter, as shown.

    MOM_force once S M_spindle M_range X Y Z F

  11. On the Operation Start Sequence tab, from the list, select New Block.

  12. Drag the new block to the Initial Move marker, after the PB_CMD_spindle_rpm_range_select block, and before the PB_CMD_spindle_rpm_check command block, if it is present.

  13. Click the new block to edit it, and add the following words:

    • M_range (M41 – Separate Range M Code (M41, M42, ,,,)

    • S (S – Sp,,)

    • M_spindle (M03 – Spindle Direction(CLW/CCLW))

  14. Right-click the new S M03 M41 block and choose Copy As→Referenced Block(s).

  15. Under the First Move marker, right-click the PB_CMD_spindle_rpm_range_select block and choose Paste→After.

    Every operation has either a first move or an initial move, but never both.

    16. The resulting output resembles the following:

    T03 M06
    S100 M03 M41
    G00 G90 X2.5909 Y3.0723
    ...
    T04 M06
    S300 M03 M42
    G00 G90 X2.5909 Y3.0723

Coolant On

Click the Coolant On event marker to specify M codes for the available cooling options on your machine.

A coolant M code is generated if a start event is defined in the NX operation or assigned to an object in the setup such as a tool, machine tool, carrier, and so on.

By default, the coolant code is output with the initial move at a feed rate. Be careful not to assign a word to the Coolant On event unless you want the coolant to start when the event is triggered, before the first rapid move, and possibly before the tool change.

You can trigger the coolant on event earlier by attaching it to a marker within a sequence, for example, to the Approach event marker in the Operation Start Sequence.

Custom command to output “( >>> Approach <<< )” as shown in the code example.

New block added, and the M_coolant – Coolant Code (FLOOD/MIST/ON..) word is added to the block. The Coolant Mist code is M07.

...
N0030 T03 M06
N0040 T02
( >>> Coolant On event triggered <<< )
( >>> Coolant code is: MIST <<< )
N0050 S100 M03
N0060 G00 G90 X9.9361 Y-3.4941
N0070 G43 Z.5118 H03
( >>> Approach <<< )
N0080 M07
N0090 Z.1181
( >>> Engage <<< )
N0100 G01 Z0.0 F19.7
...

Coolant event attached to operation start events

If the coolant on event is included in the start events of an operation, the coolant event occurs after the tool change, in the sequence defined by the markers. You can use the information in the coolant event in custom code or event markers after the event occurs.

By default, the coolant code is output with the first move at a feed rate, as shown in the following commented code example.

%
N0010 G40 G17 G90 G70
( >>> Start of operation: FACE_MILLING <<< )
N0020 G91 G28 Z0.0
N0030 T03 M06
N0040 T02
( >>> Coolant On event triggered <<< )
( >>> Coolant code is: ON <<< )
N0050 S100 M03
N0060 G00 G90 X9.9361 Y-3.4941
N0070 G43 Z.5118 H03
( >>> Approach <<< )
N0080 Z.1181
( >>> Engage <<< )
N0090 G01 Z0.0 F19.7 M08
...

Coolant event attached to a setup object

If the coolant event is assigned to a setup object, the coolant event occurs before the start of operation. You can access coolant data in your custom code or event markers from the start of the operation.

By default, the coolant code is output with the first move at a feed rate, as shown in the following commented code example.

...
( >>> Coolant On event triggered <<< )
( >>> Coolant code is: ON <<< )
( >>> Start of operation: FACE_MILLING_AREA <<< )
N0320 G91 G28 Z0.0
N0330 T02 M06
N0340 T03
N0350 S500 M03 M42
N0360 G00 G90 X-1.6732 Y3.0723
N0370 G43 Z-3.9997 H02
( >>> Approach <<< )
N0380 Z-4.4722
( >>> Engage <<< )
N0390 G01 Y3.08 Z-4.4742 F19.7 M08
...

Coolant Off

Click the Coolant Off event marker to specify M code to shut coolant off on your machine.

This dialog allows you to specify how the coolant is turned off.

You can activate a colloant off event in the following ways:

  • Add a operation end event.

  • Add a Coolant Off event to the end events of a setup object such as a tool or carrier.

  • Add a M_coolant – Coolant Off block in a sequence, for example, the End of Operation sequence.

By default, the coolant off code is output at the following times:

  • Before the end of operation event, if it is included as an end event for the operation.

  • After the end of an operation where a setup object is last used, for example, after the operation that last uses a tool or carrier.

Cutcom On

This dialog allows you to define the output and parameters to activate cutter compensation. You can specify three things: the G-codes for cutter compensation to left or right of the tool path, the maximum and minimum values for the cutter compensation register and the plane code that the system outputs.

There are two basic methods for outputting the cutter compensation codes. If you select the default method, the system outputs the code on the line with the linear or circular move. In the second option, you use only this dialog to specify parameters and define G-codes. Then output all of the cutter compensation words in the linear motion block. To use this second method, you must delete all the words from this event and make sure that all these words are in the linear motion block. By default all of these codes are also in the linear motion block as well. These codes are modal and are only output once, so no duplicate output results. However, if you delete the cutter compensation codes from this dialog,only the linear motion block outputs the codes.

Cutcom Off

This dialog allows you to specify the G-code the system outputs for turning off cutter compensation. The system outputs all necessary CUTCOM/OFF UDEs automatically. However, if you want additional G40 codes output, you can add them to the appropriate sequences to insure that cutter compensation is not active.

Opstop

Use this dialog to specify the output when the system handles the UDE OPSTOP event.

Auxfun

This event marker will output an Auxiliary M code when the UDE Auxfun is output in the toolpath. The default behavior is the same as the traditional APT output. For example, an Auxfun UDE with a value of 55 will output an M55.

Prefun

This event marker outputs a Preparatory G code when the system outputs the UDE Prefun in the toolpath. The default behavior is the same as the traditional APT output. For example, the system outputs a Prefun UDE with a value of 55 as a G55.

Load Tool

This event is by default not handled and is included here only to support legacy operations. All the data needed for tool change is available in the tool change event itself and in the UDEs that are assigned to the tool, machine tool and carriers in the setup. If the LOAD/TOOL UDE needs to be handled, the blocks can be constructed in this dialog.

Stop

Use this dialog to define the output required when the system handles the UDE STOP event.

Tool Change Mill

This dialog defines the parameters for the Tool Change function. The system uses parameters defined here in other sequences.

You can modify the blocks the system outputs by adding, editing or deleting words to the Tool Change blocks in the Automatic Tool Change event of the Start of Operation sequence.

位于何处？

Select Program and Tool Path-→ Program-→ Tool Change.

M Code

These M codes can be used in as many blocks as desired. Note that when you change the value of one of the M codes, in will change the value in other blocks.

Tool Change

Defines the M code used to index the turret. You must place a block containing the M code word in the Tool Change Event in the Operation Sequence. By default, the M code word is placed in the Tool Change Event.

T Code

Configure

Select this button and the system displays the Tool Code Configuration dialog.

Tool Number

Minimum

Defines the minimum tool number that this machine uses.

Maximum

Defines the maximum tool number that this machine uses.

Time

Tool Change

In this field enter the amount of time you want added to each tool change.

Retract Z Position

Specify a Z position for the tool to rectract to for each tool change. The system uses this Z position in configuring subsequent work plane changes. This is useful if your M06 command causes the machine tool to retract. This way the post processor knows the location of the tool.

Feedrates Mill

This dialog allows you to define how the feedrates is output on your three axis mill. You can specify the feedrate mode G-code that determines the mode (IPM, FRN) that your mill outputs. For the feedrate mode G-codes to output, the word must be included in every motion block. The default three axis mill post processors do not output feedrate mode G codes. You can also define the formats and the maximum and minimum feedrate values that are valid within each mode. See the format section for details on how to specify and edit formats.

Your post processor outputs IPM mode when you specify either an IPR feedrate or an IPM feedrate for a specific motion. You can only get inverse time feedrates (FRN) output on a three axis mill by specifying the UDE SET/MODE,INVERS. To turn off FRN output mode, use the UDE SET/MODE,OFF. If your post processor needs the G94/G93 codes output, you need to add the Feedrate Mode Word to each motion block event.

You can control whether the cycle feedrates are in IPM or IPR (MMPM or MMPR) with the Cycle Feed Rate Mode option. The choices are IPM, IPR or Auto (MMPM, MMPR or Auto).

IPM or IPR

force a constant feedrate output mode. The IPR value specified in NX is converted to the desired feed rate based on the current spindle RPM.

Auto

outputs the feedrate mode set in the feeds dialog in NX.

Feedrates Mill 4/5 Axis

This dialog allows you to configure how your post processor controls the feedrates for the linear and rotary axes. You can use this dialog for indexing as well as contouring rotary axes. You can define the feedrate mode G-codes, formats and maximum and minimum feedrates for each of the feedrate modes. See the Format section for details on how to specify and edit formats.

The default four and five axis post processors include the G93/G94 in all motion blocks. If you do not need these codes you can either edit all of the motion blocks and remove the feedrate mode G-code word from each block or go to the Word Sequencing dialog to deactivate it globally.

You can specify the feedrate mode for all combinations: whether there is a linear move in a motion block, whether there is a rotary move in a motion block and whether the move is a rapid positioning move or a linear cutting move. It is very common to have all of the options set to IPM mode for positioning tables. For full five axis machine a common requirement is to set all the linear output modes to FRN and all of the positioning output modes to IPM. Many controls also need to have DPM (Degrees Per Minute) mode for moving the rotary axes.

You can control whether the cycle feedrates are in IPM or IPR (MMPM or MMPR) with the Cycle Feed Rate Mode option. The choices are IPM, IPR or Auto (MMPM, MMPR or Auto).

IPM or IPR

force a constant feedrate output mode. The IPR value specified in NX is converted to the desired feed rate based on the current spindle RPM.

Auto

outputs the feedrate mode set in the feeds dialog in NX.

You can select the output mode for any type of motion:

Motion

Output mode

Linear only

IPM or FRN

Linear and Rotary

IPM, FRN, or DPM

Rotary only

IPM, FRN, or DPM

Rapid Linear only

IPM or FRN

Rapid Linear and Rotary

IPM, FRN, or DPM

Rapid Rotary only

IPM, FRN, or DPM

The variable $feed contains the actual value in the F word. The variable $feed_mode determines whether a G93 or G94 code is output. The content of feed_mode is either IPM, FRN, or DPM.

Expression in the F word address: $feed

Expression in the G_feed word address: $mom_sys_feed_rate_mode_code($feed_mode)

See the Machine Tool section for specifying the DPM and rotary axes parameters.

Tool Change Lathe

This dialog allows you to define the parameters that relate to a tool change for a lathe.

M Code

These M codes can be used in as many blocks as desired. Note that when you change the value of one of the M codes, in will change the value in other blocks.

Tool Change

Defines the M code used to index the turret. You must place a block containing the M code word in the Tool Change Event in the Operation Sequence. By default, the M code word is placed in the Tool Change Event.

Primary Turret

Defines the M Code to designate the primary turret if more than one turret is needed. By default this code is not output. You must place it in a block in the Tool Change Event in the Operation Sequence.

Secondary Turret

Defines the M Code to designate the secondary turret if more than one turret is needed. By default this code is not output. You must place it in a block in the Tool Change Event in the Operation Sequence.

Tool Number

Minimum

Defines the minimum tool number that this machine uses.

Maximum

Defines the maximum tool number that this machine uses.

T Code

Primary tool index

Defines an integer that the system can output with the tool number and adjust register number for the primary turret.

Secondary tool index

Defines an integer that the system can output with the tool number and adjust register number for the secondary turret.

Time

Allows you to define the time in seconds to be added to total machine time for the tool change.

If you select the Tool Code Configure button, you can specify the format of the Turret Index, Tool Number and Length Offset Number. Notice that the system displays the current format in the upper portion of the dialog. Note that you must insure that the number of digits defined in your T word is sufficient to contain the constructed T code. The following formats and the corresponding options are available:

T01

Tool Number Only

T0102

Tool Number and Length Offset Number

T11

Turret Index and Tool Number

T1102

Turret Index, Tool Number and Length Offset Number

Feedrates Lathe

This dialog allows you to define how the system controls feedrates for lathes. You can specify the feedrate mode G-code that determines the mode (IPM, IPR) that your lathe outputs. For the feedrate mode G-codes to output, the word must be included in every motion block. The default lathe post processors include this as basic functionality. You can also define the format and the maximum and minimum feedrate value that is valid within each mode. See the format section for details on how to specify and edit formats. The post processor will also give a warning if the IPR value and the current spindle RPM value exceed the maximum IPM value or is less than minimum IPR value.

By default your post processor outputs IPR mode when you specify an IPR feedrate for a specific motion and outputs IPM mode when you specify an IPM feedrate for a specific motion. The UDE that controls this function is the SET/MODE command. SET/MODE,OFF is the default mode and means that the feedrate output mode is the same as the feedrate mode for that motion. SET/MODE,IPM means that all feedrate output is output in IPM mode regardless of the input feedrate mode. Likewise SET/MODE,IPR means that all feedrate output is output in IPR mode regardless of the input feedrate mode. You can also get inverse time feedrates (FRN) output by specifying the UDE SET/MODE, FRN.

Delay Lathe

This dialog defines the output for causing a dwell to occur on your lathe machine tool. You can define the G-code for seconds or revolution mode and the format of the dwell code for both seconds and revolution mode. You have the following options for the dwell output mode.

Seconds Only

this mode will only use the seconds G-code and seconds format for all dwell output

Revolutions Only

this mode will only use the revolutions G-code and seconds format for all dwell output

Depends On Feedrate

this mode will use the seconds G-code and seconds format when in feedrate IPM mode and will use the revolutions G-code and seconds format when in IPR mode for all dwell output

线切割 Specific

Thread Wire

This event occurs when the system encounters a LOAD/WIRE UDE command. By default 后处理构造器 outputs an M code with the specified thread code.

Cut Wire

This even occurs when the system encounters an UNLOAD/WIRE command. By default 后处理构造器 outputs an M code with the specified cut wire code.

Wire Guides
