This event occurs at the start of the operation to define the position of the upper and lower wire guides. You can specify the UDE SET/Upper, z1, LOWER, z2 in the Start Post UDEs to define the positions. Z1 and z2 are the numbers defining the upper and lower positions.

Delay

This event marker allows you to define the output when the system outputs a Delay UDE in the tool path.

Feed Rate

This dialog allows you to define the maximum and minimum feed rates allowed on your wire EDM machine. Feed rates are not valid on most wire EDM machine tools. The validity and format of feed rates varies depending on the specific control.

Flushing On

This event occurs when you specify a FLUSH/ON UDE command.

Flushing Off

This even occurs when you specify a FLUSH/OFF command.

Power On

This even occurs when you specify a POWER/ON UDE command.

Power Off

This even occurs when you specify a POWER/OFF UDE command.

Motion

The Motion dialogs describe how your post processor handles the GOTO records in the tool path. The system generates more types of motion generated than most machine controls can output. The Rapid move event handles all the motion the system generates that contains a feed rate of zero. This includes retract, approach, departure, return, rapid, and traversal. If any of these motion types have a non-zero feed rate, then the system uses the Linear Move event. The system uses the Linear Move event for all the cutting motion types. These include cut, engage, first cut, stepover, and side cut. All circular motion is handled by the Circle Move event. Note that a threading pass usually consists of a Linear Move (infeed), the threading motion itself (Lathe Thread Move), the pullout move (linear or rapid depending on the feed rate) and the traversal move (linear or rapid depending on the feed rate). Several user defined events ( GOHOME and RETRCT) also generate motion. They use the Rapid Move event to output. The Nurbs event will be handled in a future release of 后处理构造器.

The motion dialogs contain codes such as S, M03, M08, G41, G17, and D. This way the system outputs these codes with motion. The system outputs these codes only if the data changes or they are being forced out with alignment. The user defined event (COOLNT/FLOOD) defines the data for turning the coolant on. This event occurs either before the Start of Operation or in its sequence in the Start Post commands, depending on how the event was specified. Output the coolant in the first G01 block before you start cutting. This is the most reliable method. See the Machine Control section for more information on the recommended methods of outputting machine control information.

The motion dialog shows you a summary of the output for each of the lathe motion types. You can click any one of the blue buttons and edit that motion event.

Linear Move

Use this dialog to define the output for linear motion. You can change the linear motion G code with this dialog. With this event dialog you can add additional blocks or words to your motion block. If you need the F code output before the motion block, simply drag the F word until it highlights above the block.

If you select the Assumed Rapid Mode at Maximum Traversal Feed check box you get a G00 move instead of a G01 move during traversal motion when the feed rate is the maximum that the axes being moved can handle.

Warning:

Assumed Rapid Mode at Maximum Traversal Feed can cause a rapid dog-leg move during the traversal motion. Use it with extreme caution.

Circular Move

The Circular Move event dialog allows you to define the parameters and blocks for outputting circles. This dialog allows you to define the

G code (clockwise or counterclockwise), that the system outputs with circle block. If you need the G02 or G03 output with every block then right click on the G02 word and set the option to force output. You can do this with any word in any block in 后处理构造器.

You can set the output to

full circle or output only

quadrant boundaries. With full circle output you get a circle block for each 360 degrees of the circle. If the input file has a circle that is 400 degrees, you will get one block at 360 degrees and another with the endpoint at 400 degrees. If you set the output to quadrant boundaries you get a circle block for each quadrant boundary and another circle block with the endpoint. Note that you use the same circle event block for each quadrant you output.

You can also define the

minimum and maximum radius that the system outputs in a circle record. If a circle is processed that has a radius less than the minimum or greater than the maximum, the system approximates the circle with linear moves using the circle tolerance.

The dialog for the

mill Circular Move has an added capability of outputting circles in other planes. You can specify that you want XY plane only, YZ plane only, ZX plane only or all three planes.

You can also define how the system specifies the circle center. The choices on the dialog define what the system outputs with the I and K words. If you need to output the radius instead of I and K, you can find the address you need from the list of addresses as shown above. Pull down the address arrow until you see the R word and select the Arc Radius. Now drop the R word into your block. If you don't need I, K, and R, then remove the I and K and put them in the trash can.

If you manually edit the expression in the I, J or K words, the system retains the edit and uses the setting in the circle center dialog. To correct the word to the desired format, set the circle center setting to the desired option, right click on the address and select Change Element. Select the arc center option from the list. It is now parameterized again and the output for the word will be correct. If the option is grayed out, then it is already set correctly.

Rapid Move Mill

The Rapid Move dialog for Milling defines the G code and output for the Rapid Move event for milling machines with a spindle axis along the Z-axis. The system uses this template for all rapid moves in your post processor. This event handles all non-cutting move motion types.

The rap1 and rap2 words represent the motion perpendicular to the spindle axis. On a typical three axis milling machine, rap1 represents the X axis and rap2 represents the Y axis. The rap3 word represents the motion along the spindle axis. The rap3 word typically represents the Z axis.

When you select the Work Plane Change check box, the post tries to make a "safe" move. The display changes and there are two blocks in the dialog. If the direction of motion along the tool axis is positive along the spindle axis, the system outputs the block with rap3 first, followed by the block with rap1 and rap2. If the direction of motion is negative along the spindle, the system outputs the block with rap1 and rap2 first followed by the block with rap3.

When you clear the Work Plane Change check box, the system outputs the rap1, rap2 and rap3 words in the same block. The display changes and there are only single blocks in the dialog.

Note:

Note that if you switch back and forth between Work Plane Change On and Work Plane Change Off some of the words you may have added may be missing or placed on a different block.

Rapid Move Lathe

The Rapid Move dialog for lathe defines the G code and output for the Rapid Move event for two axis lathes. The system uses this template for all rapid moves in your post processor. This event handles all of the motion types that are not cutting moves.

Special Rapid Motion Formatting

Some controls require special codes in the rapid motion block. For instance, the Bostomatic control requires a G01 and R in the rapid motion block. To configure your rapid motion to output this way, follow the steps below:

  1. Select the address pull down and select the word Text.

  2. Enter the character R.

  3. Add the word to your rapid move block.

  4. Go to the G code tab and change the G code from 0 to 1 for rapid motion.

Another example is a control that requires an F0 in addition to a G00 in the rapid motion block. To configure this block, follow the steps below:

  1. Select the address pull down and select F and the phrase user defined expression.

  2. Enter the value 0.

  3. Add the word to your rapid move block.

Lathe Thread

The Lathe Thread event dialog allows you to define the output for a single threading pass. For turn threads the system outputs a block with G33 Z and K and for face threads it outputs a block with G33 X and I. For taper threads the system outputs a block with G33 X Z I and K. You can also set the G codes for increasing and decreasing lead threads.

The capability for a Thread cycle that includes feed, thread, pullout, and traverse in a single canned cycle thread block is not currently available. A thread cycle event is not yet available from NX.

Canned Cycles

This dialog allows you to configure the output canned drilling cycles. 后处理构造器 allows you to define all the parameters necessary to format the canned cycles for your machine tool. The main Canned Cycles dialog has several blue markers that allow you to define your canned cycle output.

There are two types of cycles in the Canned Cycles folder:

  • System user defined cycles are displayed in light blue. System user defined cycles are not defined in the ude.cdl file unless new parameters are added.

  • Non-system (custom) user defined cycles are displayed in green. By default, custom user defined cycles are not defined in the ude.cdl file unless new cycles are added.

When you create a new cycle, the event definition is added to the .CDL file with the correct syntax, and the event handler is added to the .tcl file.

位于何处？

Menu

Options→Enable UDE Editor→Yes (once for each post)

Location in dialog box

Program & Tool Path tab→Program tab

Canned Cycles folder

Edit an existing cycle

Right-click the cycle and choose Edit User Defined Parameters.

Create a new cycle

User defined cycles are applicable only for hole making operations in mill posts.

Right-click in the background and choose Create New Custom User Defined Cycle.

Cycle Parameters

The marker Common Parameters allows you to define the parameters that apply to each cycle. Once you have defined the behavior of the approach to the hole, feed to depth and retract from the hole in the Common Parameters dialog, the system applies them to all canned cycles. If you set up your canned cycles to use a definition block followed by G79, it applies all cycles. You can disconnect the global behavior of any cycle by selecting the customize button to left of each cycle. Once you select customize, you can then make each cycle independent.

This common parameters dialog allows you to define and display how your canned cycles behave. You can define the G code that the system uses with each of the pre-defined cycles. You can also modify them using the G-code dialog. You can also define how the system outputs each element of the canned cycle. Some cycles contain incremental feeds (DEEP, BRKCHP) and others may have a programmed dwell at the bottom of the hole (DRILL, DWELL; BORE, DWELL; BORE, MANUAL, DWELL).The tool then either feeds or rapids up depending upon the cycle. After the system completes the cycle you can program an additional retract to the RTRCTO plane. The following options can be set as common parameters: Cycle Start, Rapid To, Retract To, Feed To, Cycle Plane Control, and Additional Cycle Parameters.

Cycle Start

Most canned cycles are defined and executed with a single block. You can define how your canned cycles are defined and executed.

G81 X Y Z R F

This causes a hole to be drilled at location XY. However, some controls need a definition block and an execution block (Cycle Start Block).

G81 Z R F

G79 X Y

The G79 block causes the machine to drill a hole at location XY. If you only need a G79 XY block, you can remove the Z from the G79 block. If you require a different G code, you can modify the G code.

Rapid To

This parameter allows you to define how your canned cycle block handles the RAPTO or minimum clearance distance. Depending on how your canned cycles are configured there are a number of choices you can select.

  • R The default and most common setting is to output R in the canned cycle block. The post adds the rapid to clearance distance along the tool axis to the top of the hole position.

  • None The setting of None for the Rapid To parameter is useful if your machine uses cams or has manual control of the drilling cycles.

  • Rapid Traverse & R With the option of Rapid Traverse & R the machine performs a rapid positioning move to a point above the hole and then performs the drilling cycle. This option splits the cycle into multiple blocks.

  • Rapid Use the setting of Rapid for machines such as the Bridgeport that do not have an R word in the canned cycle. The post must position in X, Y and Z above the hole to the rapid clearance plane. The canned cycle itself only feeds in Z.

Retract To

This parameter defines how your post processor handles the RTRCTO parameter in a canned cycle block. The RTRCTO parameter, when specified, causes an additional retraction after the canned cycle is completed. The RTRCTO position is determined by adding the RTRCTO parameter to the top of the hole position along the spindle axis. The following options are available in 后处理构造器 in order for you to define your output.

  • None The option None means that the system ignores the parameter. The post processor assumes that the canned cycle controls this function.

  • K The option K outputs a K word in the canned cycle block. Kearney & Trecker controls specifically use this option.

  • G98/G99 The setting of G98/G99 includes a G98 or G99 code in each canned cycle block. Fanuc controls uses this option to determine whether the tool retracts to the R plane or the last non cycle Z position before the canned cycle. The parameter RTRCTO,MANUAL causes the post to output a G99 and the parameter RTRCTO,AUTO causes the post processor to output a G98.

    To use the G98/99 functionality from GPM/MDFG in Postbuilder:

    1. Add the G_return (G98/G99) word address to your cycle block.

    2. Set the retract type in common cycle parameters to Rapid Spindle.

    3. Program RTRCTO.

      Programming for RTRCTO

      Result in cycle block

      AUTO

      G98

      value entered

      G99 followed by a G00 Z in the next block

      None

      G99

  • Rapid Spindle The setting of Rapid Spindle causes the post processor to output a rapid move along the tool axis after each canned cycle to the retract to position.

  • Cycle Off then Rapid Spindle The setting of Cycle Off then Rapid Spindle causes the post processor to cancel the canned cycle and then output a rapid move along the tool axis after each canned cycle to the retract to position.

Feed To

This parameter allows you to specify how the depth of the canned cycle can be configured. Normally in the common cycles parameter the canned cycle depth parameter is the Z word.

The default cycle depth defaults to mom_cycle_feed_to_pos(2). The system calculates this to mom_pos(2) + mom_cycle_feed_to. You can change the expression that calculates the value of the Z word by following the steps below:

  • Select the Z word by right clicking.

  • Select Change Element and User Defined Expression.

  • Enter an expression that describes how the cycle depth is calculated.

Machine tool controllers use the common expressions for depth of hole explained below.

Absolute Depth

$mom_cycle_feed_to_pos(2)

Incremental Depth from Top of Hole

$mom_cycle_feed_to

Incremental Depth from Clearance Plane

$mom_cycle_feed_to - $mom_cycle_rapid_to

Positive Incremental Depth from Top of Hole

abs($mom_cycle_feed_to)

Positive Incremental Depth from Clearance Plane

$mom_cycle_rapid_to - $mom_cycle_feed_to

Cycle Plane Control

  • None The post does not automatically output a G17, G18 or G19.

  • G17/G18/G19 The post outputs G17, G18 or G19 with the canned cycle block.

Additional Cycle Parameters

You can customize each of the cycle types to output additional words. For instance, the deep cycle can have additional specifiers for step, step increment, etc. Note that the parameter must be specified in your canned cycle for the word to be available. You can select this word from the word selection pull down. The following addresses can be selected from the word selection pull down.

  • cycle delay The delay value is in seconds. It is available in any canned cycle with the word DWELL in it.

  • cycle_step This is step #1 in the cycle parameter dialog.

  • cycle_step1 This is step #2 in the cycle parameter dialog.

Another method that you can use to output additional words is to specify a user defined event to a tool or operation and use the mom variable you have created in the canned cycle.

Cycle Plane Change

NX outputs an event (MOM_cycle_plane_change) to allow you to process work plane changes. A Work Plane Change occurs when the next tool position is higher, relative to the spindle axis, than the current position. The system uses the event mark Cycle Plane Change to output a move, if desired, to a higher plane before the next cycle is executed. You may attach a block or call a custom command to output the work plane change move. The mom_variable, mom_cycle_clearance_pos defines the X, Y, Z position.

Cycles

Cycle Off

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_off event occurs.

Drill

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_drill_move event occurs.

Drill Dwell

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_drill_dwell_move event occurs.

Drill Deep

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_drill_deep_move event occurs.

Drill Break Chip

This marker allows you to specify what the systm outputs when the MOM_cycle_drill_break_chip_move event occurs.

Tap

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_tap_move event occurs.

Bore

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_bore_move event occurs.

Bore Drag

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_bore_drag_move event occurs.

Bore Nodrag

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_bore_nodrag_move event occurs.

Bore Manual

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_bore_manual_move event occurs.

Bore Dwell

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_bore_dwell_move event occurs.

Bore Back

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_bore_back_move event occurs.

Bore Manual Dwell

This marker allows you to specify what the system outputs when the MOM_cycle_bore_manual_dwell_move event occurs.

Simulated Cycles

For machines that do not have canned cycles, you can do the following to output your drilling cycles with linear and rapid moves.

  • Under Program and Tool Path → Custom Command → Import select the custom command pb_cmd_simulated_cycles.tcl and click OK.

  • Under Program and Tool Path → Program → Tool Path → Canned Cycles → Common Parameters, for each cycle you want to simulate, select the customize button.

  • Under Program and Tool Path → Program → Tool Path → Canned Cycles for each cycle you want to simulate, select the appropriate marker.

  • Remove all the words in the event dialog.

  • On the word selection pull down under command, select the cycle that matches the event. It is in the form PB_CMD_sim_cycle_drill_move.

  • Click on Add Word and drag the custom command to the dialog.

The custom command PB_CMD_initialize_spindle_axis may be modified if your cycle spindle axis is not along the positive Z axis.

The system generates all simulated cycles along the spindle axis. For tilting head four and five axis machines, the system performs the cycles along the tool axis.

All motion is output through the PB_CMD_linear_move.

If you want to customize the simulated cycles you may edit any of the custom commands in the custom command pb_cmd_simulated_cycles.tcl.

Miscellaneous

Use the events on the Miscellaneous page to output specific codes for suboperations in generic motion or probing operations.

  • The Start of Subop Path event in NX triggers the MOM_start_of_subop_path event handle in postprocessing.

  • The End of Subop Path event in NX triggers the MOM_end_of_subop_path event handler in postprocessing.

For Siemens 840D Lathe and Siemens 840D Millturn posts there are two additional events to handle the output of contour data. Contour data, which describes the shape of the part, is generated for the Siemens Cycle95 lathe roughing cycle when you set Machine Control→Motion Output to the Machine Cycle option.

  • The Contour Start event in NX triggers the MOM_contour_start event handler in postprocessing.

  • The Contour End event in NX triggers the MOM_contour_end event handler in postprocessing.

Operation End Sequence

The operation End Sequence allows you to specify certain blocks to come out at the end of every operation. Typically these are things like returning to a home position and turning off the spindle or coolant. If you know you are always going to do the same end of operation functions at the end of every operation, you can enter these into this sequence instead of programming UDEs. Note that some operations do not have a tool change and you might want to handle the end of operation differently depending on whether the next operation has a tool change.

Mill and Lathe

Return Move

This event only occurs if you have programmed a return point in the avoidance in the operation. If you attach a block to this marker, the system only outputs the block if you have programmed a return move in the operation.

Gohome Move

This event only occurs if you have programmed a gohome point in the avoidance control module. If you attach a block to this marker, the system only outputs the block in which you have programmed the gohome move.

线切割

Lead Out Move

This even occurs only when you specify a lead out move in the operation.

End of Pass

This event occurs at the end of each roughing or finishing pass from the 线切割 module in NX.

Gohome Move

This event occurs when you specify a GOHOME move in the wire EDM module avoidance control.

End of Path

This even occurs at the end of each operation.

Program End Sequence

This dialog allows you to define the output at the end of every NC program.

Note that the Start of Program and End of Program events are only output once by the main post of linked post. If you overwrite either of these event handlers, the linked post reinstates the original Start of Program and End of Program.

Add a new block

You can create a new block in the following ways:

  • You can drag a new block on the Program tab of the Program & Tool Path tab.

  • You can copy an existing block on the Block tab of the N/c Data Definitions tab.

This procedure describes how to create a block in the Program tab of the Program & Tool Path tab.

  1. On the Program tab, select one of the following sequences:

    • Program Start Sequence

    • Operation Start Sequence

    • Operation End Sequence

    • Program End Sequence

    In the Tool Path sequence you can add words, and events if the UDE editor is enabled, but not blocks.

  2. From the list of available blocks, select the type of block to add.

  3. Drag Add Block to a position that is that reflects where you want the output.

    Position new block with highlighting here…

    …to achieve this result

Add an operator message

The example shows how to add an operator message for a manual tool change.

  1. In the program sequence tree, select Operation Start Sequence.

  2. From the list of available blocks, select Operator Message.

  3. Drag Add Block to the Manual Tool Change event marker, and drop it after the M00 (stop) block.

  4. In the edit dialog box, type Load Tool Number $mom_tool_number and click OK.

    Make certain that any variable name that you include in the message is defined when time the operation is processed. A run-time error occurs if an undefined variable is encountered. To open the Mom Variables Browser to review the list of variables that you can use, choose Utilities→Browse MOM Variables.

Copy an existing block

You can copy a block in your program sequence to create another instance of the same block, or to use as the basis for a new code block.

Create a new instance of a block

  1. Right click an existing block and choose Copy As→Referenced Block(s).

    In the graphic, the G91 G28 Z0 block is being copied.

  2. Right click a block before or after where you want a new instance, and choose Paste→Before, or Paste→After, as required.

    In the graphic, the copied block is being pasted before the selected PB_CMD_tool_change_force_addresses block.

    3. The original block and the copied block now have a light blue background to indicate that they are instances. Changes to any instance of the block affect all instances.

Copy a block to use as a new code block

  1. Right click an existing block and choose Copy As→New Block(s).

  2. Paste the copied block in the location where you require it.

    3. The new block has a white background, indicating that it represents unique code. You can edit it without affect the original block.

Apply output conditions to a block

  1. On the Program & Tool Path tab, on the Program tab, select one of the following pages:

    • Program Start Sequence

    • Operation Start Sequence

    • Program End Sequence

    • Operation End Sequence

  2. Right-click a normal code block

    You can identify a normal code block by the icon.

  3. Choose Output Condition→New.

    The procedure statement and the first part of the procedure name are completed automatically. 

    proc PB_CMD_check_block_xxx {} {
    }

    In the example, XXX represents the content of a text box. The box contains a default name based on the name of the marker under which the block appears. You can change this to a name that is meaningful to you.

  4. In the Custom Command dialog box, add the code and return conditions to control whether the block is output.

    The default code is as follows:

    # This custom command should return
    #   1 : Output BLOCK
    #   0 : No output

    global mom_logname




    return 1

    You would typically add:

    • One or more global variables to check.

    • A statement to return 0 under the conditions for which you do not want output. For example, you could use the if command or the switch command.

  5. When your Tcl coding is ready to test, click OK

G Codes tab

This section allows you to specify G codes used throughout your post processor to control various machine functions. By changing the G codes in this list, the system updates them globally.

M Codes tab

This section allows you to specify M codes used throughout your post processor. By changing the M codes in this list, the system updates them globally

Word Summary tab

This dialog allows you to define the output characteristics of each of the words that your post processor outputs. Note that this dialog modifies groups of words that use the same format. This dialog allows you to modify the format. If you want to specify a different format for one of the words, you must go to the word address section of N/C Data Definitions. The following fields are available:

Word

Contains the system's symbol for the word. This name is the same name that you find in the word address section of the N/C Data Definitions section.

Leader/Code

Lets you modify the leader for any word. The leader is the character than precedes the numeric information. For instance, if the word is X12.345, then the leader is X. You can either type in a new leader or right click on the existing leader and choose from the list.

Data Type

The data type can be either numeric or text. Usually all N/C code is numeric. Specify text when the code you need cannot be formatted from a number directly into a word address and a value. For example, if you need a G84.1 for tapping and G81 (without a decimal) for drilling. In this case you have to make the G_motion word a text and change all the G codes to G81 instead of 81 and a leader of G. You can also go to the format dialog in N/C Data Definitions to change only the G_motion word to text.

Plus (+)

Lets you output a plus sign for all positive numbers. If you set this option to No you get an X1.0. If you set this to yes, you get an X+1.0. You always get a minus sign for all numeric data if the specified number is negative.

Lead Zero

Lets you define whether or not you output leading zeroes with your N/C Code.

Integer

Controls the number of digits that are output to the left of the decimal point. You get an error if your numeric data exceeds the format you have specified.

Decimal (.)

Controls whether or not numeric data is output with a decimal point. You get an error if you suppress the decimal point and do not output at least leading or trailing zeroes.

Fraction

Determines how many digits are output to the right of the decimal point.

Trail Zero

Lets you define whether or not you output trailing zeroes with your N/C Code.

后处理构造器 automatically outputs a zero format that is compatible with the options you have chosen. If you suppressed the decimal point, your format for outputting zero is M0. If you are outputting the decimal point, your zero format is X0.0. If you want to change how your post processor outputs a value of zero for your coordinate data, see the zero option for formats in the N/C Data Definitions section.

Word Sequencing tab

This dialog allows you to define the order in which the system outputs all the words in your post processor. The system enforces the relative order of the words in this dialog throughout your entire post processor. For example, if you exchange the X and Y words, it is immediately reflected in all the blocks in all events.

You can also select or make any word active. Selecting any word changes it from pink to blue, indicating that it is now suppressed.

Output – Active

Output – Suppressed

If Balloon Help is active, each word displays the context of that word.

By default, the system uses a representative code to display that word. For example, the G17 word can also be G18 or G19. In other cases a D or T represents the adjust register or tool number register. Any change to the leader for that word is reflected in this dialog.

In this dialog you can perform the following functions:

Exchange Two Words

Select and drag one of the words you wish to exchange until the word you want to exchange it with is fully highlighted. Release the mouse and they have now exchanged places.

Place a Word Between Two Other Words

Select and drag the word you want to move until the space between the words is highlighted. Release the mouse placing your word between the other two words.

Move a Word To The Beginning Or End Of A Row

Select and drag the word you want to move until the space at the front or end of the row is highlighted. Release the mouse placing the word at the beginning or end of the row.

Custom Command tab

Custom commands generate the output that you specify. Define custom commands when the output that you require is not available. For example, you can define a custom header at the beginning of your output program. Custom procedures appear in the list of commands that you can place with any event marker on the Program tab.

When you define a custom command, you complete it by adding Tcl code. You can import the command from a provided library or define your own code. 后处理构造器:

  • Provides a default procedure name.

  • Provides the correct syntax, including the braces that enclose the body of the procedure.

  • Checks the syntax of the code you enter and displays syntax error messages for your code automatically.

Click Export to create your own library file. You can specify one or more custom commands that you can later import into other postprocessors.

You can check for unknown commands, unknown blocks, unknown addresses and unknown formats for validation by choosing Options→Validate Custom Commands.

Custom command library

The following custom commands are available in the custom command directory to add functionality to your post.

PB_CMD_abort_event

Lets you abort an event that meets the condition specified by the global variable mom_sys_abort_next_event. Linear, Circular, Rapid and Cycle move events all have this command by default. You can also use this command to abort any other event.

pb_cmd_activate_turbo_mode.tcl

This file contains custom commands that you can use to activate turbo mode in a post. Turbo mode improves post processing performance of posts for three, four and five-axis machines.

The custom command for each machine controller family is listed below:

PB_CMD__activate_Fanuc_turbo_mode

for Fanuc style controls

PB_CMD__activate_iTNC_turbo_mode

for Heidenhain iTNC controls

PB_CMD__activate_Sinumerik_turbo_mode

for Siemens Sinumerik controls

Import the appropriate custom command to your post and add it to the Start of Program event to activate the turbo mode. You can customize a command further by following the instructions documented in the command file.

PB_CMD_before_output

Lets you manipulate the NC data before it is finally output to a file. This command, when present, is automatically executed by the MOM_before_output handler.

Previously, it was acceptable to replace the MOM_before_output handler with a customized version. As of PB6.0, do not overwrite the MOM_before_output handler. Use PB_CMD_before_output for all customization.

pb_cmd_center_of_ball_output

This custom command outputs center of ball in every linear and rapid move block. To use this option, turn on the Output Contact Data button on the Machine Control dialog. This custom command is only valid for the Fixed and Variable Axis Surfacing Contouring modules in NX2 or later.

pb_cmd_cincinnati_pq_cutcom.tcl

Use this custom command to add PQ cutcom vectors to your post.

  • Place the command PB_CMD_pq_cutcom_initialize in the Start of Program event marker.

  • Place the command PB_CMD_cutcom_on with the Cutcom On event marker.

  • Remove the G40 from the Cutcom Off event marker, the G41 word from the linear, circular and rapid move markers.

  • Add the PB_CMD_pq_motion to the linear, circular and rapid move markers.

Alternatively you can use the pre-defined controller for the Cincinnati Acramatic control. This control has all of the above pre-defined.

pb_cmd_coordinate_system_rotation

This custom command outputs the code needed to cause a coordinate system translation and rotation on the control. Starting in NX2 a MOM_set_csys event with a coordinate system type and matrix will be output at the start of every tool path where the MCS changes. If the coordinate system type is csys, the system converts the coordinate system into the rotations and translations needed.

This custom command must be placed in the Start of Program event marker.

You can modify this custom command to output the code you need for a specific control. For Fanuc, Siemens, Heidenhain and Mazatrol, the specific code is included.

This custom command is used primarily for head/table five-axis or head/head five-axis machine tools.

When you set the type to siemens you have the following choices: fanuc, siemens, heidenhain_iso, heidenhain_conv, mazatrol. These custom commands are intended for general cases. You may find that you need to make some adjustments to get the desired output.

PB_CMD_fix_RAPID_SET

Add this command to the Start of Program event if the legacy command does not account for the –X or –Y directions. You can also use PB_CMD_fix_RAPID_SET to temporarily switch off the work plane change when the spindle axis is not along a principal direction.

pb_cmd_fixture_offset_with_G10

This custom command determines the correct information for the G10 work coordinate system definition. Place this custom command in the Start of Program event marker. The system outputs a G10 block every time the MCS changes.

You must define a main MCS in the Geometry View. This coordinate system must be coincident with machine tool coordinate system and located at the center of rotation of the fourth axis. You can then create your MCS's under the main MCS in the Geometry View.

You may need to customize the MCS dialog. If so, add the option for Coordinate System Purpose and Special Output to the MCS dialog. Set Coordinate System Purpose to MAIN for the main MCS and LOCAL for the local MCS. The Special Output should be set to FIXTURE OFFSET.

For four axis machines the coordinates systems must meet the following criteria:

For rotary axis tables in the YZ plane

the X axis of the local MCS must be parallel (not reversed) with the X axis of the main MCS

For rotary axis tables in the ZX plane

the Y axis of the local MCS must be parallel (not reversed) with the Y axis of the main MCS

For rotary axis tables in the XY plane

the Z axis of the local MCS must be parallel (not reversed) with the Z axis of the main MCS

For three axis mills

the X, Y and Z axis of the local MCS must be parallel with X, Y and Z axis of the main MCS.

Five axis mills

XZC mills and lathes are not supported with this function.

pb_cmd_helix.tcl

Use this custom command to add a handler for the event MOM_helix_move. You can also define how the system outputs the helix pitch. Place the command PB_CMD_initialize_helix at the Start of Program event marker. The helix event now uses the circular move block to output the helix block.

PB_CMD_init_turbo_output

Note:

This is a legacy command that is still supported. Newer custom commands for turbo mode are available. Please see pb_cmd_activate_turbo_mode.tcl.

Place this custom command in the start of program event marker. It will initialize turbo output mode.

The execution time will be about five to ten times faster than normal processing time. The blocks being output are similar to the Fanuc type. A special set of turbo blocks are used for the Heidenhain Conversational control.

The system will display the following turbo blocks in the Block section of N/C Data definitions:

  • linear_move_turbo

  • circular_move_turbo

  • circular_move_1_turbo (Heidenhain only)

  • rapid_move_turbo

  • sequence_number_turbo

To modify the output of a word within the turbo blocks, modify the Word parameters with the N/C Data Definitions dialog. You can also modify the Format parameters. The turbo processor uses the words and formats to output turbo blocks. However, you can not, edit the expression in the words, remove words, or add words in any turbo block.

The reason that Turbo mode is faster is that no expressions are evaluated.

All rapid motion is output in a single move. Any custom commands in the linear move, circular move, rapid move and mom_before_motion event markers are ignored by the system.

The turbo post processing mode only supports three axis mills.

Note:

  • This custom command may only be used with 后处理构造器 3.2.1 or later and NX2 or later. The Heidenhain Conversational functionality can only be used with NX3 or later.

  • Turbo mode does not support mirroring. To output the same moves in turbo and non-turbo mode:

    1. In the Machine Tool dialog box, clear the Mirror Output check boxes.

    2. In the Circular Move dialog box, select the Vector - Arc Start to Center option from the IJK Definition list.

PB_CMD_reset_output_digits

For the Sinumerik_840D template posts. Sets the number of digits for the linear axes and rotary axes output format, depending on the transformation mode and motion type.

PB_CMD_restore_work_plane_change

If you use the PB_CMD_fix_RAPID_SET command and want to restore the work plane setting, add this command at the end of a Rapid Move event.

PB_CMD_set_cycle_plane

Determines a proper principal axis by using the mom_spindle_axis vector.

PB_CMD_set_resolution

For the Sinumerik_840D template posts. Sets the resolution used for rounding coordinate data. The resolutions should be synchronized with the number of digits assigned with PB_CMD_reset_output_digits.

pb_cmd_simulated_cycles.tcl

Use this custom command to simulate any one of the standard canned cycles. You can modify the custom PB_CMD_initialize_spindle_axis to specify a spindle axis other than along the positive Z axis. See the simulated cycle section for details.

PB_CMD_Sinumerik_default_setting

Sets the default settings for Sinumerik_840D posts.

PB_CMD_Sinumerik_Version

Sets the Sinumerik version for Sinumerik_840D posts.

Sinumerik version 6 and later support CYCLE832 high speed machining G code output.

pb_cmd_spindle_rpm_range_select.tcl

This custom command automatically selects the spindle range based on the current spindle rpm. Place the command PB_CMD_ spindle_rpm_range_select in the Start of Operation sequence before you output the spindle range code for the operation. This custom command only selects the spindle range, for details on how it can be output see the section on Spindle Output.

pb_cmd_spindle_sfm_range_select.tcl

This custom command automatically selects the spindle range to output based on the current spindle sfm. Place the command PB_CMD_ spindle sfm_range_select in the Start of Operation sequence before you output the spindle range code for the operation. This custom command only selects the spindle range, for details on how it can be output see the section on Spindle Output.

pb_cmd_spindle_table.tcl

This custom command allows you to define the S code table that the system uses for each spindle rpm and range. You need to modify the custom command PB_CMD_get_spindle_table_scode for your machine. The default is nine ranges and nine S codes per range. You need to place the command PB_CMD_ spindle_table in the Start of Operation sequence before you output the spindle S code for the operation. If you have a spindle speed specified but no tool change, you must place the command in the Start Of Path event. This custom command only selects the S code, for details on how it can be output see the section on Spindle Output.

pb_cmd_tape_length.tcl

This custom command allows you to determine the actual tape length in feet. Place the custom command PB_CMD_ptp_size at the end of the End of Program event marker.

pb_cmd_three_dimensional_tool_offset

This custom command outputs 3d cutter compensation blocks for Fanuc type controls.

The initial block will look like the following:

G41 G01 X Y Z I J K D

I,J,K represent the contact normal output.

You will need to do the following to your post in 后处理构造器:

  1. Add I,J, and K words to the linear move block using the following user defined expressions for each:

    I -- $mom_contact_normal(0)

    J -- $mom_contact_normal(1)

    K -- $mom_contact_normal(2)

  2. Add the following line (without the #) to the custom command PB_CMD_before_motion

    #PB_CMD_three_dimensional_tool_offset

  3. Add the custom command PB_CMD_cutcom_on to the event marker Cutcom On under Program/Tool Path/Machine Control.

To use this command turn on the Output Contact Data button on the Machine Control dialog and turn off circular interpolation output. Then output a CUTCOM/LEFT to activate the G41 block.

This option is only valid for the Fixed and Variable Axis Surfacing Contouring modules in NX2 or later.

pb_cmd_tool_list.tcl

This custom command allows you to output a tool list either at the start or end of your nc file. Place the custom command PB_CMD_initialize_tool_list in the Start of Program event marker. Then place the custom command PB_CMD_create_tool_list either after the custom command PB_CMD_initialize_tool_list in the Start of Program event marker or in the End of Program event marker.

pb_cmd_zw_axis.tcl

This custom command allows you to control the output of parallel Z and W axis. This is typically a situation where you have quill (Z axis) that has limited travel and a table (W axis) that has more range of motion. The post keeps track of the quill and table at all times and applis the current position of the parked axis while the other one is positioning.

To use this option you must place the command PB_CMD_initialize_parallel_zw_mode in the Start of Program event marker. You can now edit the custom command PB_CMD_initialize_parallel_zw_mode and define the home position for the Z and W axis and the leader you wish to use for the quill and table. The default is Z and W.

The system contains several useful custom commands to control the Z and W axis. These commands may be placed with any event marker.

PB_CMD_set_quill_home

Positions the quill to its home position at rapid.

PB_CMD_set_table_home

Positions the table to its home position at rapid.

PB_CMD_set_mode_zaxis

The W axis (table) is parked. The Z axis (quill) will now position.

PB_CMD_set_mode_waxis

The Z axis (quill) is parked. The W axis (table) will now position

Additionally you can also use the UDE post commands SET/MODE,ZAXIS and SET/MODE,WAXIS to change to mode of output. The UDE post commands SET/ZAXIS,z and SET/WAXIS,w can also be used to position the axis.

You can also assign additional parameters to your cycle block.

Prerequisite: NX 2.0.1

  1. Create a CYCLE in your ude.cdl file

    1. Copy the following and paste it into your ude.cdl file in the user defined events folder, but be sure to remove the comments ().

    2. Replace every instance of "special" with the name of your cycle.

    3. Add as many parameters as you like.

      For example, the line "PARAM special_cycle_param_1" will generate a mom variable mom_special_cycle_param_1 when the cycle is generated. You can then use the mom variable as an expression when you add a word to your cycle block. The syntax for a CYCLE is the same as any other UDE except for CYCLE instead of EVENT. 

      CYCLE special 
      { 
      UI_LABEL "Special" 
      PARAM special_cycle_param_1 
      { 
         TYPE d 
         DEFVAL "0.0000"
         UI_LABEL "Special cycle value 1"
      }
      PARAM special_cycle_param_2 
      { 
         TYPE d 
         DEFVAL "0.0000"
         UI_LABEL "Special cycle value 2"
      } 
      }

  2. Add a cycle block in N/C Data Definitions for each new cycle.

    1. Go to the N/C Data Definitions tab.

    2. Select block.

    3. Select one of the cycle blocks, cycle drill is good.

    4. Select create.

    5. Rename the block by right clicking. Give it the name of your cycle preceded by cycle_.

    6. Right click on the G address in the block and select Change Element and then User Defined Expression.

    7. Enter the number for your G code.

  3. To add additional parameters to the block

    1. Select New Address from the pull down.

    2. Select add block and add the word to your block.

    3. Change leader, format and modality as needed. For format, use coordinate for numeric data and digit_2 or digit_4 for integer data.

    4. Enter your mom variable from the ude.cdl the expression.

    5. Go to word sequencing if your new word is not in the order you need.

  4. Add this custom command to the Start of Program event marker. If you have a linked post, this custom command must be in the main post Start of Program.

  5. Make sure the name of your cycle in the ude.cdl is the same as the name of the event handlers.

  6. Make sure the names of your cycle parameters in the ude.cdl PARAM section are the same as in the cycle block in N/C Data Definitions except preceded with mom_.

  7. There are two basic kinds of canned cycles listed here. Use the cycle start cycle for Maho, Heidenhain, etc. Use the standard one for Fanuc, Siemens, Fadal, Mazatrol, etc.

Importing custom commands

  1. Click Import.

    Library procedures usually contain comments that provide the necessary instructions and a summary of how the procedure works.

  2. In the Select a File dialog box, navigate to the provided library or to a location where you store your own custom command libraries.

    The custom command library is in the ${UGII_BASE_DIR} postbuild pblib custom_command folder. Library files begin with the prefix pb_cmd_ and have the extension .tcl.

  3. Select a file and click OK.

    The Tcl file is checked for syntax errors and for new commands.

  4. In the Import Commands from file name dialog box, select one or more procedures to import.

    Only procedure names that you do not have defined in your post are shown. Click Deselect All to clear all selections, and then double-click the ones that you want to select. Click Select All to select all procedures.

  5. Click OK to import the commands.

Export

The Export tab under Program and Toolpath→Custom Command → Export, allows you to export the custom commands you have created or modified in your post builder session to a file. The system displays a list of all non-后处理构造器 procedures allowing you to select the files you wish to export. You can then import these commands into another post processor.

You can export several custom command procedures into a single file. This makes it easy to export and import groups of procedures.

Macro tab

Use this tab to create, edit, or remove block templates for making calls to macro, cycle, or functions in the NC program.

The output codes are in the form of cycle or macro calls, such as CYCLE81(...) for the Sinumerik 840D controller or CYCLE DEF 204 A200=... for the Heidenhain iTNC530 controller, or other similar high level function calls.

Adding macros

后处理构造器 provides constructs for well-known hole-making cycles attached to appropriate events.

Add you own macros when you wish to extend the post to output macro calls for other calls that your controller accepts.

List of macros in the current post. You can create a new macro, or cut, paste, or rename macros.

Sets characters to follow macro parameter words, for example, set to none, you might see X1.234 as an output parameter. With Link Character set to Equal Sign, the output for the same value is X=1.234.

Preview of the output macro call.

Sets the format of the parameter list. You can change the separator and the start and end characters. The defaults are a comma separator. The list starts with ( and ends with ).

Textual or graphical information about the cycles of a controller may be provided by the manufacturer, for example, Siemens. Click to display the info page for the macro or cycle, if available.

Right-click to set the word character for each parameter in the macro call. Right-click the outer edges of the box to cut and paste parameters. The New right-click option inserts a row after the selected parameter.

Sets the output word for the macro or cycle call.

Sets the expression for the value of parameters. Choose a common expression or edit the expression as required.

Sets the format for the parameter output as a numeric format or text string.

Notes

The additional output attributes are not applicable to the Siemens Sinumerik 840D. The options do not appear on the macro constructor for the Sinumerik family of posts.

You can define as many parameters as your controller requires.

If a macro parameter is not to be output, its name is used only in 后处理构造器 to represent an available argument.

You can set the expression for each parameter using constants, global MOM variables, or other Tcl variables. The variables you use should represent data of the same type as specified by the Data Type option, the same number format, or text.

Your macros for the current post appear in the list of available blocks on the Program tab.

You can create new macros by choosing Custom Macro from the list of available blocks. The tree list does not appear; otherwise, the interface is the same.

On the Program tab, when you attach your macro to an event, you can suppress the sequence number.

Macro tab

Use this tab to create, edit, or remove block templates for making calls to macro, cycle, or functions in the NC program.

The output codes are in the form of cycle or macro calls, such as CYCLE81(...) for the Sinumerik 840D controller or CYCLE DEF 204 A200=... for the Heidenhain iTNC530 controller, or other similar high level function calls.

Adding macros

后处理构造器 provides constructs for well-known hole-making cycles attached to appropriate events.

Add you own macros when you wish to extend the post to output macro calls for other calls that your controller accepts.

List of macros in the current post. You can create a new macro, or cut, paste, or rename macros.

Sets characters to follow macro parameter words, for example, set to none, you might see X1.234 as an output parameter. With Link Character set to Equal Sign, the output for the same value is X=1.234.

Preview of the output macro call.

Sets the format of the parameter list. You can change the separator and the start and end characters. The defaults are a comma separator. The list starts with ( and ends with ).

Textual or graphical information about the cycles of a controller may be provided by the manufacturer, for example, Siemens. Click to display the info page for the macro or cycle, if available.

Right-click to set the word character for each parameter in the macro call. Right-click the outer edges of the box to cut and paste parameters. The New right-click option inserts a row after the selected parameter.

Sets the output word for the macro or cycle call.

Sets the expression for the value of parameters. Choose a common expression or edit the expression as required.

Sets the format for the parameter output as a numeric format or text string.

Notes

The additional output attributes are not applicable to the Siemens Sinumerik 840D. The options do not appear on the macro constructor for the Sinumerik family of posts.

You can define as many parameters as your controller requires.

If a macro parameter is not to be output, its name is used only in 后处理构造器 to represent an available argument.

You can set the expression for each parameter using constants, global MOM variables, or other Tcl variables. The variables you use should represent data of the same type as specified by the Data Type option, the same number format, or text.

Your macros for the current post appear in the list of available blocks on the Program tab.

You can create new macros by choosing Custom Macro from the list of available blocks. The tree list does not appear; otherwise, the interface is the same.

On the Program tab, when you attach your macro to an event, you can suppress the sequence number.

Inherit UDE definitions and handlers

The definitions and handlers of the user defined events defined in a post can be inherited by another post. This capability is used by the on-machine probing operations to control the probing cycles.

To inherit the definitions and handlers defined from another post:

  1. Click the N/C Data Definitions tab.

  2. In the User Defined Events group, select the Inherit UDE From A Post check box.

  3. Enter the post name and folder location or click Select Post.

Preview definitions and handlers

To preview definitions, click Output Settings→Post Files Preview→User Defined Events.

To preview handlers, click Output Settings→Post Files Preview→Event Handlers.

NC Data Definitions

Use the NC Data Definitions tab to define output formats for NC data.

Words

Words are the basic unit of data. Words are typically, but not always, a letter followed by one or more digits, for example, G01, X100.35, and M03 are typical words. We refer to the initial letter as the word address.

You can select a word from a list by its group name and name. For example G_motion→G01–Linear Move, X→X–X Coordinate, or M_Spindle→M03–CLW Spindle Direction appear in lists. You can choose words from a tree diagram by their name. For example, G_motion, X, and M_Spindle appear in the word tree diagram.

Word names in 后处理构造器 sometimes refer to several possible related controller words, for example, the following post words can identify several controller words:

  • G_Cutcom – Cutcom(LEFT/RIGHT/OFF) can output G41, G42, or G43, as required by the event generated in the tool path.

  • M_Spindle – Spindle Direction(CLW/CCLW) can output M03 or M04, as required.

Blocks

Blocks consist of one or more words arranged in the context of a machine operation. The order of words in a block is set on the Word Sequencing tab. You can drop a word into a block anywhere, and it assumes the correct position.

Blocks also have names that you can select from lists or tree diagrams.

For example:

  • The default cutcom_off block contains the single word G_cutcom – Cutcom Off and outputs G40 by default.

  • The default absolute_mode block contains the words G_cutcom – Cutcom Off, G_plane – XY Plane Code, G_mode – Absolute Mode, and G – Output Unit (Inch/Metric), and the default output for a metric post is G40 G17 G90 G71.

Formats

Formats are also named and you can select a word format from a list or a tree diagram.

There are many default named formats such as Coordinate, Digit_2, and Tcode.

Block tab

Use the Block tab to create and edit blocks. When you create a new block on this page, you are copying an existing block and editing it. You are limited to copying a single existing block. If you need more than one block for your function, you must create multiple blocks on the Program tab of the Program & Tool Path tab.

Creating a New Block

You can create a new block in the following ways:

  • Drag a new block into a sequence on the Program tab of the Program & Tool Path tab.

  • Copy an existing block on the Block tab of the N/C Data Definitions tab.

Editing new or existing blocks

You can edit a block in the following ways:

Add a new word

Select applicable words from the list, and drag Add Word to the block.

Remove a word

Drag unwanted words to the icon, or right-click and choose Delete.

Modify a word

Right-click a word and choose from the following actions:

Edit

Opens the dialog box to edit a word.

Change Element

Lets you swap the word for another word in the same group. For example, you can replace G17 with G18.

Optional

When a check-mark appears, the word is not output if a variable for the word is not defined, and there will not be an error message.

Click the menu option to toggle it between selected and not selected.

G90 is required — there are no conditions turned on

G90 with the optional condition turned on

No Word Separator

When selected. omits the separator, for example a space character, before the word.

Click the menu option to toggle it between selected and not selected.

P with the optional condition turned on

P with the optional and no word separator conditions turned on

Force Output

Available in blocks where output is not forced by the presence of the word.

The word is output, regardless of modality, provided the optional setting does not inhibit the output.

G41 with no conditions turned on

G41 with force output turned on

G41 with all conditions turned on

Delete

Removes the word from the block.

Preview a word format

Examine the green box above the word parameter window to preview the format of the selected word.

Restore a previous state

Choose one of the following:

Default

Restores the block being edited to the state last saved with the post.

Restore

Restores the block being edited to the state it was in when you began the current edits.

If you stop editing a block, edit something else, and come back to it later, Restore does not remove earlier edits in the session.

When you right-click a word in a block, the menu contains a mixture of commands and status indicators, as described in the preceding paragraphs. The status of the three indicators is shown graphically by the word in the block, as described, and also by the presence of a check mark. In the following graphic, the Optional status indicator is checked, meaning that the optional output condition is turned on.

New Address

Select New Address from the list and drag Add Word to a block. The WORD: user_add dialog box appears, where you can name the word, set the format, and identify the expression for which your word reflects the value.

Text

Select the Text option from the list and drag Add Word to a block. The Text Entry dialog box appears

Existing Words

Select the word that you want from the list.

When you choose an existing word the current value for the associated variable is output. For example, if you choose X-Coordinate, the word is output with the current X position in the format defined for the word.

You can specify a specific variable that you create using the User Defined Expression option that is available in every word group.

Word tab

Use the Word tab to create and edit words.

A word is composed of an address leader, a number, or text and a trailer.

You have the same options for editing a word as you do within the event or sequence dialogs.

To create a new word or word group, you must copy an existing word or word group and edit the copy.

Format

You can specify the format for a selected word in the following ways.

  • Select from the list of currently defined named formats

  • Create a new format.

  • Edit an existing format and verify that the change does not compromise an existing word.

The green rectangle above the parameter input area shows a preview of the output word formatting.

Leader and Trailer

An address leader may be any characters. For NC code the address leader is usually a single letter such as G, M, or X.

Leaders and trailers can be multiple characters such as A1=, B1=, C1=, and so on for Sinumerik 840D controllers.

An example of leaders and trailers for some Siemens controls is the format for absolute output of circle centers with I, J, and K.

G3 X-3.257 Y-47.45 I=AC(2.487) J=AC(-51.45)

Leader

Trailer

I=AC(
)
J=AC(
)
K=AC(
)

The same is true for some tool numbers, expressed as TC(1):

Leader

Trailer

TC(
)
Modal?

Select Yes for words that are modal. The word is output only if the value of the expression changes. This is the default setting for all words.

Typically, modal words are for coordinate data, motion G codes, and so on.

Select No for words that must be output in every block in which the word appears.

For example, the opstop event should always force an M01; the Modal? option is set to No by default.

Maximum and Minimum

Define the maximum and minimum value that can be output with the selected word.

You can define the action when maximum or minimum value is exceeded from the following:

Truncate

The maximum or minimum value is used instead of the value specified in NX.

Warning

Warnings are output if one of the following is true:

  • The Output Warnings postprocessing option in NX is set to Yes.

  • The 后处理构造器 option Output Warning Messages on the Output Settings→Other Options tab is selected and the Output Warnings option in NX is set to anything other than No.

Warnings are output to a file with the base name of the output file, and the suffix and extension warning.out.

For example, if the output file is my_part.ptp, the warning file is my_part_warning.out.

There is no interactive warning in the NX session.

Abort

Postprocessing is aborted. An error message is output to the system log file, Help→Log File.

The message may appear at the beginning of a lengthy trace, but you can identify the beginning lines similar to the following:

*** IKS has been initialized ***
*** EXCEPTION: Error code  1745005 in line 560 of 
o: ugnx80 ip19 src cams no ind mom_post.c at 
Tue Apr 19 11:45:16 2011 Eastern Daylight Time
+++ ADDRESS:F, Maximum is 600.000000, 
this value is 619.685039

Format tab

Use the Format tab to create and edit formats. 后处理构造器 comes with a default set of formats that are applied to words in the default posts. You can also change formats within the context of the words and blocks on several other tabs where words are displayed.

Tree diagram

The tree diagram at the left of the Format tab shows the formats that are defined in the current post. You can edit a format by selecting it in the tree.

You can create a new format by selecting an existing format and clicking Create. This gives you a new format exactly like the original, with _1 appended to the name.

You can rename, cut, and paste formats, with the exception that you cannot cut a format that is in use.

Preview

The green area at the top of the format window shows a preview of the selected format.

Data Type

Specifies the data type as numeric or text.

You should select numeric for all NC data except for certain G codes. For instance, if your controller needs L instead of G01, R instead of G00, CW instead of G02, and so on, then you can change the data type to text and change all of the codes to the text that the controller recognizes. This is the only case we have found where it is necessary to use a text format.

If you specify text, there are no further formatting options.

Note:

You should select text format only if you are familiar with this area.

Number spin boxes

Let you set the number of digits to the left and right of the decimal point.

Output Decimal Point (.)

Some words do not require a decimal point, even when the value has one or more decimal places. For example, if you want to output P3000 for a dwell time of 3.000 seconds, you would make the format 1.3, select the Output Trailing Zeros check box, and clear the Output Decimal Point (.) check box.

Check boxes

The check boxes on the Format tab do what their names indicate. Some examples follow. The number to be output is 53.1, and the spin boxes are set to the 5.3 format.

Check boxes selected

Output

Output Decimal Point (.)

53.1

Output Decimal Point (.)
Output Leading Plus Sign (+)

+53.1

Output Decimal Point (.)
Output Leading Zeros

00053.1

Output Decimal Point (.)
Output Leading Zeros
Output Trailing Zeros

00053.100

Output Leading Plus Sign (+)
Output Leading Zeros
Output Trailing Zeros

+00053100

If none of the decimal point, leading zeros, or trailing zeros check boxes is selected, you will get an error message. At least one of these must be selected to assure that the output has a clear meaning.

When the output represents a value of zero, then the following formats are used:

Zero_int

Outputs integer numbers with leading zeros.

Zero_real

Outputs decimal numbers with both leading and trailing zeros.

Other Data Elements tab

Use the Other data Elements tab to set or specify the following properties.

Sequence Number

Lets you specify the starting number, increment between numbers, and the frequency of sequence numbers per line of output. A frequency of one, for example, outputs a sequence number on every line, while a frequency of 5 outputs a sequence number only on every fifth line.

If necessary, you can also specify the maximum sequence number that can be used before starting again at the first number.

Special Characters

Lets you define the following characters:

Word Separator

Lets you specify one of several common characters that controllers recognize as word boundaries.

You can also specify Text String.

For example, choose Space, the default, for the following pattern in a line of output:

N0010 G01 X1.23

If you choose Colon, the same block would be output as:

N0010:G01:X1.23
End of Block

Lets you specify one of several common characters that controllers recognize as the end of a block or line of output.

You can also specify Text String.

Message Start and Message End

Let you specify one of several common characters that controllers recognize as the start and end of comments or operator messages.

OPSKIP

Lets you specify in the Line Leader box the character that the controller uses to skip execution of blocks if the controllers’ operation skip button is turned on.

The default character is / (the forward slash).

User Defined Events

When Include Other CDL File is selected, you can do the following.

  • Right-click the background of the window and choose New to browse to a .cdl file that contains events that you want to have available in your post. There are some library files available in the ...resource user_def_event folder, for example, Siemens_Cycles_plus_ude.cl, or ude_millturn_lathe.cdl.

  • Double-click a file name, or right-click a name and choose Edit, to change the folder or file that is referenced.

  • Right-click a line and choose Up, Down, Cut, or Paste to arrange the order in which .cdl files are read. This is important, because if there are events with the same name in different files, the version of the event in the last file in the list is the one that is used.

Inherit UDE From A Post

When Inherit UDE From A Post is selected, you can do the following.

  • Right-click the background of the window and choose New to browse to a post that contains events that you want to have available in your post.

  • Double-click a file name, or right-click a name and choose Edit, to change the folder or file that is referenced.

  • Right-click a line and choose Up, Down, Cut, or Paste to arrange the order in which posts are read. This is important, because if there are events with the same name in different posts, the version of the event in the last post in the list is the one that is used.

Include Own CDL File

When Include Own CDL File is selected, the Folder box becomes available.

The default contents of the box is $UGII_CAM_USER_DEF_EVENT_DIR/, typically your ... MACH resource user_def_event folder.

Format sequence numbers to have a decimal

This example demonstrates how to create a new format as you edit a word, and shows where to set parameters for sequence numbering in your output.

  1. On the N/C Data Definitions tab, click the Word tab.

  2. From the list of blocks, select N.

  3. Under Format, click New.

  4. In the Format Name box, enter a name for the format, for example, decimal_seqno, or some other name that identifies the purpose of the format.

  5. Under Data Type, select Numeric.

  6. Select Output Decimal Point.

  7. Specify the decimal format you want, for example, 4.1.

  8. Select any leading or trailing zero options you want.

  9. In the Format dialog box, click OK.

  10. On the N/C Data Definitions tab, select the Other Data Elements tab.

  11. Under Sequence Number, set the parameters for start, increment, and frequency, and if necessary, for the maximum sequence number.

Create a UDE or UDC in 后处理构造器

The procedures for creating UDEs and UDCs are very similar. The procedure for a new UDE is illustrated in this example.

  1. In 后处理构造器, on the Program & Tool Path tab, click the Program tab.

  2. Under Tool Path, select one of the following pages:

    • Machine Control for a new UDE.

    • Canned Cycles for a new UDC.

  3. Right-click in the background of the page and choose the applicable command.

    The command on the Machine Control page is Create New Machine Control Event.

  4. In the Create Machine Control Event dialog box specify the required event parameters and any optional parameters that you want to include.

  5. Click OK.

  6. (Optional) In the User Defined Event dialog box, drag parameter type buttons, one at a time, to your event dialog box.

    For each parameter that you drag, a dialog box lets you enter information for the parameter type that you selected.

Output Settings tab

Use the Output Settings tab to control elements such as following:

  • The presence of a listing file and details included in the listing.

  • The way your post handles program groups in NX.

  • The defaults for warning output and the debugging tool.

  • Additional Tcl files to read before posting.

  • Links to Alternate Units subposts.

You can also compare what is as stored on disk to information currently in memory for the post you are editing, for the following categories:

  • Events

  • Formats

  • Words

  • Blocks

Listing File tab

Generate Listing File

When selected, your post always outputs a listing file.

The listing file contains a header that contains all the runtime information that includes time and date, part name, name of the postprocessor files used and user name.

The listing file also contains the NC file, warnings, and the absolute axis positions.

Listing File Extension

Sets the extension used for the listing file. The name of your listing file is the name of the output NC file, with the listing file extension. For example, for 1234.ptp, the default listing file is 1234.lpt.

Components

Use the check boxes to control the following outputs to a table in the listing file:

  • X-Coordinate

  • Y-Coordinate

  • Z-Coordinate

  • 4th Axis Angle

  • 5th Axis Angle

  • Feed

  • Speed

Other Options tab

Output Control Elements group
NC File Output Extension

Sets the default extension for the NC output file that the post generates. You can override this in NX, by entering an extension in the File Extension box in the Output File group of the Postprocess dialog box.

Generate Group Output

Lets you define how your post handles program groups in NX. You can define more than one NC program in a part file. If the top level group is NC_PROGRAM and all the subgroups are in the level immediately under NC_PROGRAM, all subgroups are output in the same NC tape file, regardless of the setting in the post. The option controls whether additional files are output.

By default, the Generate Group Output check box is not selected. All group organization in NX is ignored, and a single file is output for the selected program or program group, or for selected operations in the same group.

Select this check box when you want additional output of a separate NC tape file for each group immediately below the top level selected, for example, all top level groups below NC_PROGRAM. Each file has the same name as the top-level output file, with the group name appended to it.

For example, if the name of the NX file is gearbox.prt and the NC_OPERATION program has the following subgroups: ROUGHING, FINISH_MILLING and DRILLING, the output files for the NC_OPERATION group when the Generate Group Output check box is selected are:

  • gearbox.ptp

  • gearbox_roughing.ptp

  • gearbox_finish_milling.ptp

  • gearbox_drilling.ptp

The behavior when the check box is selected is similar if groups have subgroups below them.

Caution:

Do not mix operations in groups with operations outside of groups when the Generate Group Output check box is selected. This causes unpredictable output.

You can use the name of the selected group or program in NX as the program name in the Start of Program event. Access the name using the variable mom_group_name.

Output Warning Messages

Outputs warnings to a warning log file when Output Warnings in the NX Postprocess dialog box is set to its default value, Post Defined. The system suffix and extension _warning.out are appended to the base name of the NC output file, and the warnings file is in the same directory as the NC output.

Display Verbose Error Messages

Displays detailed error messages if a Tcl error is encountered. If you select the Display Verbose Error Messages check box when you are debugging your post; you need not examine the NX log file for error messages.

Caution:

Because this option causes degradation in performance, use this option only when you are debugging your post.

Activate Review Tool

Opens the debugging dialog box after postprocessing is complete.

The debugging dialog box has three lists. The first list displays all events that were created by the event generator. The second list displays all of the variables that were created and passed to your postprocessor during a selected event, including MOM variables, and the variable values. The third list displays a list of the NC output that your postprocessor generated.

For example, if you select an event in the first list , the data generated by the selected event is shown in the second list , and the NC code generated for the selected event is highlighted in the third list .

The listing at the top of the display shows you the selected event, or the event that caused selected NC code .

Note:

The review tool causes a significant degradation in performance. Use this option only with relatively small files and when you are debugging a problem.

Enable Advanced Turbo Post Output

后处理构造器 allows you to easily convert any regular three- to five-axis milling postprocessor to run in advanced turbo mode. The advanced turbo mode uses standard blocks and custom commands, and can improve postprocessing performance by up to 80% over the standard postprocessing mode.

Advanced turbo mode is active when the postprocessor is saved. The option loads several custom commands that are executed automatically at post runtime. The custom commands start with PB_CMD__AdvTurboPost__ and can be further customized if necessary.

Note:

Use this option instead of the standard turbo mode.

User Tcl Source

Identifies an additional Tcl file to read based on the options in this section. This file must be in the default postprocessor folder.

Source User’s Tcl File

Includes handlers for UDE post commands that you create in your own Tcl file. Your Tcl file must be in the same folder as the current postprocessor.

File Name

Specifies the name of the file that you want sourced into your post.

Optional Alternate Units Subpost

The options under Optional Alternate Units Subpost work together with the option Units Only Subpost in the Create New Post Processor dialog box.

Default

Modifies the postprocessor file name to the format for a units-only subpost. The name links your post to a subpost with millimeter units for an inch-unit post and subpost with inch units for a millimeter-unit post. You can explicitly create the subpost later, after your post is fully defined. You can also link to an existing subpost, if, for example you are linking a family of posts for a complex machine to a single subpost.

For example, if the post you are creating is named new_post and has inch units, the default name for the alternate units post is new_post__MM.pui. If the post you are creating is named my_post and has millimeter units, the default name for the alternate units subpost is my_post__IN.pui. In the name of the alternate units post, the unit abbreviation is in uppercase and it is preceded by two underscores.

If the alternate units subpost exists and is in the same folder as your post, it is used automatically when you request output in the units of the subpost.

Specify

Lets you specify an existing units-only subpost to link to your main post. The subpost must have the correct units, and must be compatible for the machine tool for which you are defining your post. For example, if your main post has millimeter units, the subpost you select must be for inch output.

Typically, you use this option to reuse the same units-only subpost for a set of linked posts, such as the set of posts for a complex machining center.

Select Name

Available when Specify is selected.

Lets you navigate to and select an existing subpost. The subpost must have the correct units.

Link Tcl Variables to C Variables

Enables NX Post to process the MOM Tcl variables more efficiently. This option can improve the performance of NX Post by approximately 25 percent; however, it might cause NX Post to fail in some situations.

Caution:

Use this option with caution, and, as always, thoroughly test your postprocessor before distributing it.

Post Files Preview tab

Use the Post Files Preview tab to examine the current and previously saved source code for the definition file and event handlers.
